title: Tokugawa Ieyasu

* [Lady Tsukiyama](/wiki/Lady_Tsukiyama "Lady Tsukiyama") * [Asahi no kata](/wiki/Asahi_no_kata "Asahi no kata")

* *Concubines*: * [Lady Saigō](/wiki/Lady_Saig%C5%8D "Lady Saigō") * [Lady Chaa](/wiki/Lady_Chaa "Lady Chaa") * [Okaji no Kata](/wiki/Okaji_no_Kata "Okaji no Kata") * [Lady Acha](/wiki/Lady_Acha "Lady Acha") * *among others...*

* [Matsudaira Nobuyasu](/wiki/Matsudaira_Nobuyasu "Matsudaira Nobuyasu") * [Kamehime](/wiki/Kamehime "Kamehime") * [Toku-hime](/wiki/Tokuhime_(1565%E2%80%931615) "Tokuhime (1565–1615)") * [Yūki Hideyasu](/wiki/Y%C5%ABki_Hideyasu "Yūki Hideyasu") * [Tokugawa Hidetada](/wiki/Tokugawa_Hidetada "Tokugawa Hidetada") * [Matsudaira Tadayoshi](/wiki/Matsudaira_Tadayoshi "Matsudaira Tadayoshi") * [Takeda Nobuyoshi](/wiki/Takeda_Nobuyoshi "Takeda Nobuyoshi") * [Matsudaira Tadateru](/wiki/Matsudaira_Tadateru "Matsudaira Tadateru") * [Matsudaira Matsuchiyo](/wiki/Matsudaira_Matsuchiyo "Matsudaira Matsuchiyo") * [Matsudaira Senchiyo](#Children) * [Tokugawa Yoshinao](/wiki/Tokugawa_Yoshinao "Tokugawa Yoshinao") * [Tokugawa Yorinobu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Yorinobu "Tokugawa Yorinobu") * [Tokugawa Yorifusa](/wiki/Tokugawa_Yorifusa "Tokugawa Yorifusa") * Furihime * Matsuhime * Ichihime * *among others...*
* [Matsudaira Hirotada](/wiki/Matsudaira_Hirotada "Matsudaira Hirotada") (father) * [Odai no Kata](/wiki/Odai_no_Kata "Odai no Kata") (mother)
* Matsudaira Jirōsaburō Motonobu (松平 次郎三郎 元信) * Matsudaira Kuradonosuke Motoyasu (松平 蔵人佐 元康) * Matsudaira Ieyasu (松平 家康)

* [Matsudaira clan](/wiki/Matsudaira_clan "Matsudaira clan") * [Imagawa clan](/wiki/Imagawa_clan "Imagawa clan") * [Oda clan](/wiki/Oda_clan "Oda clan") * [Toyotomi clan](/wiki/Toyotomi_clan "Toyotomi clan") * [Eastern Army](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara") * [Imperial Court](/wiki/Imperial_Court_in_Kyoto "Imperial Court in Kyoto") * [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate")

* [Siege of Terabe](/wiki/Siege_of_Terabe "Siege of Terabe") * [Siege of Marune](/wiki/Siege_of_Marune "Siege of Marune") * [Siege of Kaminogō](/wiki/Siege_of_Kaminog%C5%8D_Castle "Siege of Kaminogō Castle") * [Mikawa Ikkō-ikki rebellion](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%89%E6%B2%B3%E4%B8%80%E5%90%91%E4%B8%80%E6%8F%86 "ja:三河一向一揆")   + [Battle of Batogahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Azukizaka_(1564) "Battle of Azukizaka (1564)") * [Tōtōmi Campaign](/wiki/Siege_of_Kakegawa "Siege of Kakegawa") * [Battle of Anegawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Anegawa "Battle of Anegawa") * [Battle of Mikatagahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Mikatagahara "Battle of Mikatagahara") * [Battle of Nagashino](/wiki/Battle_of_Nagashino "Battle of Nagashino") * [Suruga Campaign](/wiki/Suruga_Province#Medieval_period "Suruga Province") * [Siege of Takatenjin](/wiki/Siege_of_Takatenjin_(1581) "Siege of Takatenjin (1581)") * [Battle of Tenmokuzan](/wiki/Battle_of_Tenmokuzan "Battle of Tenmokuzan") * [Tenshō-Jingo war](/wiki/Tensh%C5%8D-Jingo_war "Tenshō-Jingo war") * [Battle of Komaki and Nagakute](/wiki/Battle_of_Komaki_and_Nagakute "Battle of Komaki and Nagakute") * [Siege of Odawara](/wiki/Siege_of_Odawara_(1590) "Siege of Odawara (1590)") * [Kunohe Rebellion](/wiki/Kunohe_Rebellion "Kunohe Rebellion") * [Sekigahara Campaign](/wiki/Sekigahara_Campaign "Sekigahara Campaign")   + [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara") * [Osaka Campaign](/wiki/Siege_of_Osaka "Siege of Osaka") * *see below*

(松平 竹千代), he was the son of [Matsudaira Hirotada](/wiki/Matsudaira_Hirotada "Matsudaira Hirotada") (松平 広忠), the [daimyo](/wiki/Daimyo "Daimyo") of [Mikawa](/wiki/Mikawa_Province "Mikawa Province") of the [Matsudaira clan](/wiki/Matsudaira_clan "Matsudaira clan"), and [Odai no Kata](/wiki/Odai_no_Kata "Odai no Kata") (於大の方; Lady Odai), the daughter of a neighbouring [samurai lord](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai"), [Mizuno Tadamasa](/wiki/Mizuno_Tadamasa "Mizuno Tadamasa") (水野 忠政). His mother and father were step-siblings. They were 17 and 15 years old, respectively, when Takechiyo was born.[[7]](#cite_note-McLynn_230-11)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Japanese Samurai, Daimyo and Military ruler of Japan from 1603 to 1605

For the 1983 Japanese television series, see [*Tokugawa Ieyasu* (TV series)](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu_(TV_series) "Tokugawa Ieyasu (TV series)").

**Tokugawa Ieyasu**[[a]](#cite_note-3)[[b]](#cite_note-6) (born **Matsudaira Takechiyo**;[[c]](#cite_note-7)[[d]](#cite_note-8) January 31, 1543 – June 1, 1616) was a Japanese [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai"), [daimyo](/wiki/Daimyo "Daimyo") and the founder and first *[shogun](/wiki/Shogun "Shogun")* of the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate") of Japan, which ruled from 1603 until the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") in 1868. He was the third of the three "Great Unifiers" of Japan, along with his former lord [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") and fellow [Oda](/wiki/Oda_clan "Oda clan") subordinate [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi"). The son of a minor [daimyo](/wiki/Daimyo "Daimyo"), Ieyasu once lived as a hostage under daimyo [Imagawa Yoshimoto](/wiki/Imagawa_Yoshimoto "Imagawa Yoshimoto") on behalf of his father. He later succeeded as daimyo after his father's death, serving as ally, vassal, and general of the [Oda clan](/wiki/Oda_clan "Oda clan"),[[5]](#cite_note-:0-9) and building up his strength under Oda Nobunaga.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-10)

After Oda Nobunaga's death, Ieyasu was briefly a rival of Toyotomi Hideyoshi, before declaring his allegiance to Toyotomi and fighting on his behalf. Under Toyotomi, Ieyasu was relocated to the [Kanto](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region") plains in eastern Japan, away from the Toyotomi power base in [Osaka](/wiki/Osaka "Osaka"). He built [his castle](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle") in the fishing village of [Edo](/wiki/Edo_(Tokyo) "Edo (Tokyo)") (now [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo")). He became the most powerful daimyo and the most senior officer under the [Toyotomi](/wiki/Toyotomi_clan "Toyotomi clan") regime. Ieyasu preserved his strength during Toyotomi's failed attempts to [conquer Korea](/wiki/Imjin_War "Imjin War"). After Hideyoshi's death and the [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara"), Ieyasu seized power in 1600.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-9)

He received appointment as *shōgun* in 1603, and voluntarily resigned from his position in 1605, although he still held the [de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto") control of government until his death in 1616. He implemented a set of careful rules known as the *[bakuhan](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate#Government "Tokugawa shogunate")* system. This system used precisely graded rewards and punishments to encourage (or compel) the daimyo and samurai to live in peace with each other under the Tokugawa shogunate.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[6]](#cite_note-:1-10)

## Early life (1543–1561)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life (1543–1561)")]

Tokugawa Ieyasu was born in [Okazaki Castle](/wiki/Okazaki_Castle "Okazaki Castle") on the 26th day of the twelfth month of the eleventh year of [Tenbun](/wiki/Tenbun "Tenbun"), according to the [Japanese calendar](/wiki/Japanese_calendar "Japanese calendar"), January 31, 1543 according to the Western calendar. Originally named **Matsudaira Takechiyo** (松平 竹千代), he was the son of [Matsudaira Hirotada](/wiki/Matsudaira_Hirotada "Matsudaira Hirotada") (松平 広忠), the [daimyo](/wiki/Daimyo "Daimyo") of [Mikawa](/wiki/Mikawa_Province "Mikawa Province") of the [Matsudaira clan](/wiki/Matsudaira_clan "Matsudaira clan"), and [Odai no Kata](/wiki/Odai_no_Kata "Odai no Kata") (於大の方; Lady Odai), the daughter of a neighbouring [samurai lord](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai"), [Mizuno Tadamasa](/wiki/Mizuno_Tadamasa "Mizuno Tadamasa") (水野 忠政). His mother and father were step-siblings. They were 17 and 15 years old, respectively, when Takechiyo was born.[[7]](#cite_note-McLynn_230-11)

During the [Muromachi period](/wiki/Muromachi_period "Muromachi period"), the Matsudaira clan controlled a portion of Mikawa Province (the eastern half of modern [Aichi Prefecture](/wiki/Aichi_Prefecture "Aichi Prefecture")). Ieyasu's father, [Matsudaira Hirotada](/wiki/Matsudaira_Hirotada "Matsudaira Hirotada"), was a minor local warlord based at Okazaki Castle who controlled a portion of the [Tōkaidō](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_(road) "Tōkaidō (road)") highway linking [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto") with the eastern provinces. His territory was surrounded by stronger and predatory neighbors, including the [Imagawa clan](/wiki/Imagawa_clan "Imagawa clan") based in [Suruga Province](/wiki/Suruga_Province "Suruga Province") to the east and the Oda clan to the west. Hirotada's main enemy was [Oda Nobuhide](/wiki/Oda_Nobuhide "Oda Nobuhide"), the father of [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga").[[8]](#cite_note-Turnbull-I-12)

In the year of Takechiyo's birth, the Matsudaira clan split. Hirotada's uncle, Matsudaira Nobutaka defected to the Oda clan. This gave Oda Nobuhide the confidence to attack Okazaki. Soon afterwards, Hirotada's father-in-law died, and his heir, [Mizuno Nobumoto](/wiki/Mizuno_Nobumoto "Mizuno Nobumoto"), revived the clan's traditional enmity against the Matsudaira and declared allegiance to Oda Nobuhide as well. As a result, Hirotada divorced [Odai-no-kata](/wiki/Odai_no_Kata "Odai no Kata") and sent her back to her family.[[8]](#cite_note-Turnbull-I-12) Hirotada later remarried to different wives, and Takechiyo eventually had 11 half-brothers and sisters.[[7]](#cite_note-McLynn_230-11)

### Hostage life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Hostage life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Okazakijo2.JPG/250px-Okazakijo2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Okazakijo2.JPG)

[Okazaki Castle](/wiki/Okazaki_Castle "Okazaki Castle"), the birthplace of Tokugawa Ieyasu

As Oda Nobuhide continued to attack Okazaki, Hirotada turned to his powerful eastern neighbor, [Imagawa Yoshimoto](/wiki/Imagawa_Yoshimoto "Imagawa Yoshimoto"), for assistance. Yoshimoto agreed to an alliance under the condition that Hirotada send his young heir to [Sunpu Domain](/wiki/Sunpu_Domain "Sunpu Domain") as a hostage.[[8]](#cite_note-Turnbull-I-12) Oda Nobuhide learned of this arrangement and had Takechiyo abducted.[[9]](#cite_note-Turnbull-13) Takechiyo was five years old at the time.[[10]](#cite_note-screech85-14) Nobuhide threatened to execute Takechiyo unless his father severed all ties with the Imagawa clan. However, Hirotada refused, stating that sacrificing his own son would show his seriousness in his pact with the Imagawa. Despite this refusal, Nobuhide chose not to kill Takechiyo, but instead held him hostage for the next three years at the [Honshōji Temple](/wiki/Anj%C5%8D "Anjō") in [Nagoya](/wiki/Nagoya "Nagoya"). It was rumored that Oda Nobunaga met Takechiyo at the temple, when Takechiyo was six years old, and Nobunaga was 14. However, Katsuhiro Taniguchi reported there is no concrete historical records about this story of first meeting between Takechiyo with Nobunaga.[[11]](#cite_note-15)

In 1549, when Takechiyo was six,[[10]](#cite_note-screech85-14) his father Hirotada died of unknown causes. There was a popular theory that he was murdered by his vassals, who had been bribed by the Oda clan. However, a scholarly article published by Muraoka Mikio in 2015 states that the assassination theory is unreliable and Hirotada's death may have been caused by a natural illness.[[12]](#cite_note-muraoka-16)

Around the same time, Oda Nobuhide died during an epidemic. Nobuhide's death dealt a heavy blow to the Oda clan.

In 1551, an army under the command of [Imagawa Sessai](/wiki/Sessai_Ch%C5%8Dr%C5%8D "Sessai Chōrō") laid siege to the castle where Oda Nobuhiro, Nobuhide's illegitimate eldest son, was living. Nobuhiro was trapped by the Imagawa clan but was saved through negotiation by Oda Nobunaga, Nobuhide's second son and heir. Sessai made an agreement with Nobunaga to take Takechiyo back to Imagawa, and he agreed. Takechiyo, now nine years old, was taken as a hostage to Sunpu.

At Sunpu, he was treated fairly well as a potentially useful ally of the Imagawa clan until 1556 when he was 14 years old.[[10]](#cite_note-screech85-14) Yoshimoto decided that the Matsudaira clan's territory would be inherited by Takechiyo in the future, with the aim that the Imagawa clan could rule the area by extensions of their Matsudaira clan as their vassal, including Zuien-in (the daughter of Matsudaira Nobutada and Takechiyo's great-aunt), who was the only member of the Anjo Matsudaira clan left in Okazaki Castle.[[13]](#cite_note-17)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOishi2019134–141-18)[[e]](#cite_note-20)

### Service under Imagawa clan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Service under Imagawa clan")]

Main articles: [Siege of Marune](/wiki/Siege_of_Marune "Siege of Marune"), [Battle of Okehazama](/wiki/Battle_of_Okehazama "Battle of Okehazama"), and [Siege of Kaminogo Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Kaminogo_Castle "Siege of Kaminogo Castle")

In 1556, Takechiyo officially came of age, with Imagawa Yoshimoto presiding over his *[genpuku](/wiki/Genpuku "Genpuku")* ceremony. Following tradition, he changed his name from Matsudaira Takechiyo to **Matsudaira Jirōsaburō Motonobu** (松平 次郎三郎 元信). He was also briefly allowed to visit [Okazaki](/wiki/Okazaki_Castle "Okazaki Castle") to pay his respects to the tomb of his father, and receive the homage of his nominal retainers, led by the *[karō](/wiki/Kar%C5%8D "Karō")* [Torii Tadayoshi](/wiki/Torii_Tadayoshi "Torii Tadayoshi").[[8]](#cite_note-Turnbull-I-12)

One year later, at the age of 15 (according to [East Asian age reckoning](/wiki/East_Asian_age_reckoning "East Asian age reckoning")), he married his first wife, [Lady Tsukiyama](/wiki/Lady_Tsukiyama "Lady Tsukiyama"), a relative of Imagawa Yoshimoto, and changed his name again to **Matsudaira Kurandonosuke Motoyasu** (松平 蔵人佐 元康). A year later, their son, [Matsudaira Nobuyasu](/wiki/Matsudaira_Nobuyasu "Matsudaira Nobuyasu"), was born. He was then allowed to return to Mikawa Province. There, the Imagawa ordered him to fight against the Oda clan in a series of battles.[[16]](#cite_note-21)

Motoyasu fought his first battle in 1558 at the [siege of Terabe](/wiki/Siege_of_Terabe "Siege of Terabe"). The lord of Terabe, [Suzuki Shigeteru](/w/index.php?title=Suzuki_Shigeteru&action=edit&redlink=1 "Suzuki Shigeteru (page does not exist)") (or [Suzuki Shigetatsu](/w/index.php?title=Suzuki_Shigetatsu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Suzuki Shigetatsu (page does not exist)")[[jp](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E9%88%B4%E6%9C%A8%E9%87%8D%E8%BE%B0_(%E6%97%A5%E5%90%91%E5%AE%88) "jp:鈴木重辰 (日向守)")]), betrayed the Imagawa by defecting to Oda Nobunaga. This was nominally within Matsudaira territory, so Imagawa Yoshimoto entrusted the campaign to Motoyasu and his retainers from Okazaki. Motoyasu led the attack in person, but after taking the outer defences, he burned the main castle and withdrew. As anticipated, the Oda forces attacked his rear lines, but Motoyasu was prepared and drove off the Oda army.[[17]](#cite_note-Turnbull-I2-22)

He then succeeded in delivering supplies during the siege of [Odaka Castle](/wiki/%C5%8Ctaka_Castle "Ōtaka Castle") a year later. Odaka was one of five disputed frontier forts under attack by the Oda clan, and the only one that still remained under Imagawa control. Motoyasu launched diversionary attacks against the two neighboring forts and, when the garrisons of the other forts came to assist, Motoyasu's supply column was able to reach Odaka.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurnbull1998215-23)

By 1559, the leadership of the Oda clan had passed to Oda Nobunaga. In 1560, Imagawa Yoshimoto, leading a large army of 25,000 men, invaded Oda territory. Motoyasu was assigned a separate mission to capture the stronghold of Marune in the [Siege of Marune](/wiki/Siege_of_Marune "Siege of Marune") operation. As a result, he and his men were not present at the [Battle of Okehazama](/wiki/Battle_of_Okehazama "Battle of Okehazama") where Yoshimoto was killed in a surprise assault by Nobunaga,[[9]](#cite_note-Turnbull-13): 37 In the end, Motoyasu managed to capture Marune castle.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHonda201056-24)

Later, in response to the news of Yoshimoto's death, Motoyasu sent lookouts to check the state of the battle and then retreated from Odaka Castle at midnight. After leaving Odaka Castle, Motoyasu's forces headed for Okazaki with Asai Michitada as their guide. On the way, they were stopped by the Mizuno clan's forces at Chiryu, but because Asai Michitada was with them, they were not attacked. Having escaped from danger, Motoyasu entered Daijuji Temple outside Okazaki Castle the following day.[[20]](#cite_note-Hamada_Kōichirō_After_Okehazama-25)

With Imagawa Yoshimoto dead, and the Imagawa clan in a state of confusion, Motoyasu used the opportunity to assert his independence and marched his men back into the abandoned [Okazaki Castle](/wiki/Okazaki_Castle "Okazaki Castle") and reclaimed his ancestral seat.[[17]](#cite_note-Turnbull-I2-22) Motoyasu then decided to ally with Oda Nobunaga.[[21]](#cite_note-sam-26) Motoyasu's wife, [Lady Tsukiyama](/wiki/Lady_Tsukiyama "Lady Tsukiyama"), and infant son, Matsudaira Nobuyasu, were held hostage in [Sunpu](/wiki/Sunpu_Castle "Sunpu Castle") by [Imagawa Ujizane](/wiki/Imagawa_Ujizane "Imagawa Ujizane"), Yoshimoto's heir, so the deal was secret.[[22]](#cite_note-27)

In 1561, Motoyasu openly ended his allegiance to the Imagawa clan and captured [Kaminogō](/wiki/Siege_of_Kaminog%C5%8D_Castle "Siege of Kaminogō Castle") castle. Kaminogō was held by Udono Nagamochi. Resorting to stealth, Motoyasu forces under [Hattori Hanzō](/wiki/Hattori_Hanz%C5%8D "Hattori Hanzō") attacked under cover of darkness, setting fire to the castle and capturing two of Udono's sons. He then used them as hostages to exchange for his wife and son.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurnbull1998216-28)

On September 13th, 1561, Motoyasu sent his commanders to the battle of Fujinami Nawate, one of a series of battles between the Kira clan and Matsudaira clan over Tojo Castle, the residence of the Tojo branch of the [Kira clan](/wiki/Kira_clan "Kira clan"). In this battle, Kira Yoshiaki's chief retainer, Tominaga Tadamoto, was killed by Honda Hirotaka at Fujinami Nawate near the castle (near Seto Fujinami, Kira-cho, Nishio City, Aichi Prefecture ). Yoshiaki surrendered afterwards.[[24]](#cite_note-29)[[25]](#cite_note-30)[[26]](#cite_note-31) However, Motoyasu also lost one of his commander, Okubo Tadahō, who was killed by Tadatomo during the battle.[[27]](#cite_note-32)[[28]](#cite_note-33)

## Ieyasu-Nobunaga Alliance (1562–1582)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ieyasu-Nobunaga Alliance (1562–1582)")]

Sometime in the aftermath of the Okehazama battle where Imagawa Yoshimoto was slain, Motoyasu formed the [Kiyosu Alliance](/w/index.php?title=Kiyosu_Alliance&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kiyosu Alliance (page does not exist)") [[jp](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B8%85%E6%B4%B2%E5%90%8C%E7%9B%9F "jp:清洲同盟")] with Oda Nobunaga, daimyo lord of [Owari Province](/wiki/Owari_Province "Owari Province") and the head of the Oda clan.[[29]](#cite_note-34)[[30]](#cite_note-35)[[f]](#cite_note-38)

In 1563, Matsudaira Nobuyasu, the first son of Motoyasu, was married to Oda Nobunaga's daughter [Tokuhime](/wiki/Tokuhime_(Oda) "Tokuhime (Oda)").[[33]](#cite_note-Pitelka;_Tokugawa-39)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[g]](#cite_note-41) In February, Matsudaira Motoyasu changed his name to Matsudaira **Ieyasu**.[[33]](#cite_note-Pitelka;_Tokugawa-39)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[35]](#cite_note-Brinkley;_Tokugawa-42) Some historians believe that these actions provoked the pro-Imagawa faction, including the Sakurai and Okusa Matsudaira families, which led to the simultaneous uprising against Ieyasu in the following year.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShiba202123_quoting_"General_Overview:_Tokugawa_Ieyasu's_Movements_and_Research_in_the_Sengoku_and_Oda-Momoyama_Periods"-43)

### Unification of Mikawa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Unification of Mikawa")]

Main article: [Mikawa province](/wiki/Mikawa_province "Mikawa province")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Azukizaka_1564.JPG/250px-Azukizaka_1564.JPG)](/wiki/File:Azukizaka_1564.JPG)

[ukiyo-e](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e") print depicting the [Battle of Batogahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Azukizaka_(1564) "Battle of Azukizaka (1564)") between Tokugawa clan against [Ikkō-ikki](/wiki/Ikk%C5%8D-ikki "Ikkō-ikki") movement in 1563–1564.

During this period, the Matsudaira clan faced a threat from the [Ikkō-ikki](/wiki/Ikk%C5%8D-ikki "Ikkō-ikki") movement, in which peasants banded together with [militant monks](/wiki/S%C5%8Dhei "Sōhei") under the [Jōdo Shinshū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo_Shinsh%C5%AB "Jōdo Shinshū") sect, and rejected the traditional feudal social order. Ieyasu undertook several battles to suppress this movement in his territories, including the [Battle of Azukizaka (1564)](/wiki/Battle_of_Azukizaka_(1564) "Battle of Azukizaka (1564)").

Some of Ieyasu's vassals were in the Ikkō-ikki ranks, notably [Honda Masanobu](/wiki/Honda_Masanobu "Honda Masanobu") and [Natsume Yoshinobu](/wiki/Natsume_Yoshinobu "Natsume Yoshinobu"), who had deserted him for the Ikkō-ikki rebellion out of religious sympathy.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurnbull1998216-28) However, many of Ieyasu's core vassals who were also followers of the sect, such as Ishikawa Ienari[[37]](#cite_note-Ishikawa_Ienari-44) and [Honda Tadakatsu](/wiki/Honda_Tadakatsu "Honda Tadakatsu"), quickly abandoned the Ikkō faith of Jōdo Shinshū and stayed loyal to Ieyasu in order to confront the rebels.[[38]](#cite_note-Turnbull_2012;_Honda_Tadakatsu-45)[[39]](#cite_note-46)

On January 15, 1564, Ieyasu decided to concentrate his forces to attack and eliminate the Ikkō-ikki from Mikawa. In the [Battle of Azukizaka](/wiki/Battle_of_Azukizaka_(1564) "Battle of Azukizaka (1564)"), Ieyasu was fighting on the front lines and was nearly killed when he was struck by several bullets, but survived because they did not penetrate his armor.[[38]](#cite_note-Turnbull_2012;_Honda_Tadakatsu-45) Both sides were using new gunpowder weapons which the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") had introduced to Japan 20 years earlier. At the end of battle, the Ikkō-ikki were defeated. By 1565, Ieyasu had become the master of all of Mikawa Province.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1566, as Ieyasu declared his independence from the Imagawa clan, he reformed the order of Mikawa province, starting with the Matsudaira clan, after he [pacified](/wiki/Pacification_(military_action) "Pacification (military action)") [Mikawa](/wiki/Mikawa_Province "Mikawa Province"). This decision was made after he was counseled by his senior vassal [Sakai Tadatsugu](/wiki/Sakai_Tadatsugu "Sakai Tadatsugu") to abandon the clan's allegiance to the Imagawa clan.[[40]](#cite_note-47) He also strengthened his power base by creating a military government system for the Tokugawa clan in Mikawa which was based on his hereditary vassals, the [Fudai daimyō](/wiki/Fudai_daimy%C5%8D "Fudai daimyō"). The system which was called "*Sanbi no gunsei*" (三備の軍制) divided governance into three sections: [Hatamoto](/wiki/Hatamoto "Hatamoto")-Senshi, Higashi (western) Mikawa, and Nishi (eastern) Mikawa.[[41]](#cite_note-48)[[42]](#cite_note-49)[[43]](#cite_note-50)

### Tokugawa clan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Tokugawa clan")]

Main article: [Tokugawa clan](/wiki/Tokugawa_clan "Tokugawa clan")

In 1567, Ieyasu started the family name "Tokugawa", changing his name to the well-known **Tokugawa Ieyasu**. As a member of the [Matsudaira clan](/wiki/Matsudaira_clan "Matsudaira clan"), he claimed descent from the [Seiwa Genji](/wiki/Seiwa_Genji "Seiwa Genji") branch of the [Minamoto clan](/wiki/Minamoto_clan "Minamoto clan"). As there was no proof that the Matsudaira clan were descendants of [Emperor Seiwa](/wiki/Emperor_Seiwa "Emperor Seiwa"),[[44]](#cite_note-51) the Emperor initially did not approve the appointment, citing the lack of a precedent for the Serada clan of the Seiwa Genji clan to be appointed as *Mikawa-no-kami* (Lord of Mikawa).[[45]](#cite_note-taniguchi1994-52)

Ieyasu then consulted with imperial noble Konoe Motohisa through the mediation of a Mikawa native and the abbot of the Kyo Seiganji Temple.[[46]](#cite_note-53) Due to Motohisa's efforts, Yoshida Kaneyoshi discovered a genealogical document in the *Manri-koji* family that was precedent, saying, "Tokugawa (belongs) to Minamoto clan, as another offshoot of the Fujiwara clan," and a copy was transferred to him and used for the application.[[45]](#cite_note-taniguchi1994-52) Ieyasu then gained the permission of the Imperial Court and he was bestowed the [courtesy title](/wiki/Courtesy_title "Courtesy title") *Mikawa-no-kami* and the [court rank](/wiki/List_of_Japanese_court_ranks,_positions_and_hereditary_titles "List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles") of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (從五位下, *ju go-i no ge*).

Though the [Tokugawa clan](/wiki/Tokugawa_clan "Tokugawa clan") could claim some modicum of freedom, they were very much subject to the requests of [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga"). Ieyasu remained an ally of Nobunaga and his Mikawa soldiers were part of Nobunaga's army which captured Kyoto in 1568.

In 1568, at the same time, Ieyasu was eager to expand eastward to [Tōtōmi Province](/wiki/T%C5%8Dt%C5%8Dmi_Province "Tōtōmi Province"). Ieyasu and [Takeda Shingen](/wiki/Takeda_Shingen "Takeda Shingen"), the head of the [Takeda clan](/wiki/Takeda_clan "Takeda clan") in [Kai Province](/wiki/Kai_Province "Kai Province"), made an alliance for the purpose of conquering all the Imagawa territory.[[47]](#cite_note-Sansom_1961-54): 279 It is said[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] that the Tokugawa clan had made an agreement with the Takeda clan when dividing the territory that the eastern [Suruga Province](/wiki/Suruga_Province "Suruga Province") would become Takeda territory and the western Tōtōmi province would be Tokugawa territory, with the [Ōi River](/wiki/%C5%8Ci_River "Ōi River") as the border.

On January 8, 1569, the Takeda vassal [Akiyama Nobutomo](/wiki/Akiyama_Nobutomo "Akiyama Nobutomo") invaded the Tōtōmi province from Shinano Province. The Takeda clan, through Oda Nobunaga, with whom they had a friendly relationship, asked Ieyasu, who was Nobunaga's ally, to reconsider cooperation with the Takeda, but Ieyasu rejected the idea, demonstrating a degree of independence from Nobunaga.

### Tōtōmi campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Tōtōmi campaign")]

In 1568, Ieyasu besieged the Horikawa Castle in Tōtōmi[[48]](#cite_note-OkazakiOkubotadachika59-55) and captured it in 1569. Ieyasu then ordered Ishikawa Hanzaburo to massacre the castle prisoners and residents, including women and children. It was recorded that around 700 people were beheaded on the banks of the Miyakoda River.[[49]](#cite_note-Horikawamassacre-56) [Ōkubo Tadachika](/wiki/%C5%8Ckubo_Tadachika "Ōkubo Tadachika"), who witnessed this massacre testified in his personal journal, *Mikawa Monogatari*, that "... both men and women can be cut into pieces [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*]...".[[50]](#cite_note-57)

Later the same year, Ieyasu's troops penetrated Tōtōmi Province. Meanwhile, Takeda Shingen's troops captured [Suruga Province](/wiki/Suruga_Province "Suruga Province"), including the Imagawa capital of Sunpu. [Imagawa Ujizane](/wiki/Imagawa_Ujizane "Imagawa Ujizane") fled to [Kakegawa Castle](/wiki/Kakegawa_Castle "Kakegawa Castle"), which led Ieyasu to [lay siege to Kakegawa](/wiki/Siege_of_Kakegawa "Siege of Kakegawa").

Ieyasu then negotiated with Ujizane, promising that if Ujizane surrendered himself and the remainder of Tōtōmi, Ieyasu would assist Ujizane in regaining Suruga. Ujizane had nothing left to lose, and Ieyasu immediately ended his alliance with [Takeda](/wiki/Takeda_clan "Takeda clan"), instead making a new alliance with Takeda's enemy to the north, [Uesugi Kenshin](/wiki/Uesugi_Kenshin "Uesugi Kenshin") of the [Uesugi clan](/wiki/Uesugi_clan "Uesugi clan"). Through these political manipulations, Ieyasu gained the support of the samurai of Tōtōmi Province.[[17]](#cite_note-Turnbull-I2-22) Furthermore, Ieyasu also placed the "*Iinoya's trio"* (Iinoya-Sanninshu) of clans under the command of his trusted vassal, [Ii Naomasa](/wiki/Ii_Naomasa "Ii Naomasa").[[51]](#cite_note-shinshiro_iinoya-58) The Iinoya trio were powerful clans that originated from the eastern side of Mikawa who greatly contributed to Ieyasu's expansion during his conquest of the former Imagawa territories in Tōtōmi Province.[[52]](#cite_note-59)

In 1570, Ieyasu established [Hamamatsu](/wiki/Hamamatsu "Hamamatsu") as the capital of his territory, placing his son Matsudaira Nobuyasu in charge of [Okazaki](/wiki/Okazaki,_Aichi "Okazaki, Aichi").[[53]](#cite_note-Arms-60)[[h]](#cite_note-62)

### Campaign against Asakura-Azai clan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Campaign against Asakura-Azai clan")]

Main articles: [Siege of Kanegasaki (1570)](/wiki/Siege_of_Kanegasaki_(1570) "Siege of Kanegasaki (1570)") and [Battle of Anegawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Anegawa "Battle of Anegawa")

[Asakura Yoshikage](/wiki/Asakura_Yoshikage "Asakura Yoshikage"), the head of the Asakura clan and regent of [Ashikaga Yoshiaki](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshiaki "Ashikaga Yoshiaki"), refused to come to Kyōto. This caused Nobunaga to declare both of them rebels.[[55]](#cite_note-Sansom2-63) Several reports from *Mikawa Monogatari*, *Nobunaga Koki*, *Tokugawa Jikki*, and a supplement from *Ietada Nikki* recorded that Ieyasu and his forces also participated in Oda's punitive campaign against Asakura, in which they captured Mount Tenzutsu castle. The Oda-Tokugawa forces managed to kill 1,370 enemies on April 25.

They continued the next day by [besieging Kanegasaki castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Kanegasaki_(1570) "Siege of Kanegasaki (1570)").[[56]](#cite_note-64) However the [Azai clan](/wiki/Azai_clan "Azai clan") sent reinforcements to relieve the siege so Nobunaga retreated without contacting Ieyasu. After dawn, Ieyasu withdrew from battle guided by Kinoshita Tokichi (later named [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi")), an Oda vassal.[[57]](#cite_note-65)

Later, in July 1570, the brother-in-law of Nobunaga, [Azai Nagamasa](/wiki/Azai_Nagamasa "Azai Nagamasa"), who had broken his alliance with the Oda clan during the siege of Kanegasaki, and Asakura combined to fight the combined armies of Nobunaga and Ieyasu, who led 5,000 of his men to support Nobunaga at the battle.[[9]](#cite_note-Turnbull-13): 62 As the Oda clan engaged the Azai clan army on the right, Tokugawa's forces engaged the [Asakura clan's](/wiki/Asakura_clan "Asakura clan") army on the left. At first, Asakura's army gained the advantage as they steadily pushed back the Tokugawa army. However, Honda Tadakatsu suddenly launched a lone, suicidal charge,[[58]](#cite_note-物語日本の歴史_16:_天下びとの時代Anegawa-66) while Sakakibara Yasumasa launched his force in a timely counterattack on Asakura's flank; together they managed to beat Asakura's forces.[[9]](#cite_note-Turnbull-13) Since Ieyasu's army was now free to move, they exploited the gap between Asakura and Azai's forces and sent Tadakatsu and Yasumasa to attack the flank of Azai's formation, which allowed the Oda-Tokugawa forces to win the battle.[[59]](#cite_note-The_Samurai_A_Military_History;_Anegawa2013-67)

### Suruga campaign against Takeda clan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Suruga campaign against Takeda clan")]

Main articles: [Battle of Mikatagahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Mikatagahara "Battle of Mikatagahara") and [Battle of Nagashino](/wiki/Battle_of_Nagashino "Battle of Nagashino")

In October 1571, Takeda Shingen broke the alliance with the Oda-Tokugawa forces and allied with the [Odawara Hōjō clan](/wiki/Late_H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_clan "Late Hōjō clan"). He decided to make a drive for Kyoto at the urging of the shōgun [Ashikaga Yoshiaki](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshiaki "Ashikaga Yoshiaki"), starting by invading Tokugawa lands in Tōtōmi. Takeda Shingen's first objectives in his campaign against Ieyasu were [Nishikawa Castle](/wiki/Nishikawa_Castle "Nishikawa Castle"), [Yoshida Castle](/wiki/Yoshida_Castle_(Mikawa_Province) "Yoshida Castle (Mikawa Province)") and [Futamata Castle](/wiki/Futamata_Castle "Futamata Castle").

In 1572, after [besieging Futamata](/wiki/Siege_of_Futamata "Siege of Futamata"), Shingen would press on past Futamata towards the major Tokugawa home castle at [Hamamatsu](/wiki/Hamamatsu "Hamamatsu"). Ieyasu asked for help from Nobunaga, who sent him some 3,000 troops. Early in 1573, the two armies met at the [Battle of Mikatagahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Mikatagahara "Battle of Mikatagahara"), north of Hamamatsu. The considerably larger Takeda army, under the expert direction of Shingen, overwhelmed Ieyasu's troops and caused heavy casualties. Despite his initial reluctance, Ieyasu was convinced by his generals to retreat.[[60]](#cite_note-Cassell-68)[[53]](#cite_note-Arms-60) Later in his fortress Ieyasu brazenly ordered the men in his castle to light torches, sound drums, and leave the gates open, to properly receive the returning warriors. To the surprise and relief of the Tokugawa army, this spectacle made the Takeda generals suspicious that they were being led into a trap, so they did not besiege the castle and instead made camp for the night.[[60]](#cite_note-Cassell-68) This error allowed a band of Tokugawa soldiers to raid the camp in the ensuing hours, further upsetting the already disoriented Takeda army, and ultimately resulting in Shingen's decision to call off the offensive altogether. Takeda Shingen would not get another chance to advance on Hamamatsu, much less Kyoto, since he died from unknown causes shortly after the [siege of Noda Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Noda_Castle "Siege of Noda Castle") later that same year.[[21]](#cite_note-sam-26): 153–156

Around 1572 or 1573, according to various primary sources such as *[Shinchō Kōki](/wiki/Shinch%C5%8D_K%C5%8Dki "Shinchō Kōki")*, *Mikawa Monogatari* (三河物語), *[Ietada nikki](/wiki/Matsudaira_Ietada_(Fuk%C5%8Dzu) "Matsudaira Ietada (Fukōzu)")* (家忠日記) and *Mikawa Tōdai-ki* (三河東大記), Nobunaga sent a letter to The authorities in Suruga Province that he assigned Ieyasu as the controller of the province.[[61]](#cite_note-69)

In 1574, Shingen was succeeded by his son [Takeda Katsuyori](/wiki/Takeda_Katsuyori "Takeda Katsuyori") and the conflict continued as the Tokugawa forces under Honda Tadakatsu and Sakakibara Yasumasa seized many of the Takeda clan's castles,[[62]](#cite_note-Nagashino1573;TadakatsuYasumasa-70)[[63]](#cite_note-71) including Komyo Castle.[[64]](#cite_note-Research_establishment-72)[[65]](#cite_note-Research_of_Fudai-73) At some point, Ieyasu tried to capture Inui Castle in Tōtōmi Province, but strong resistance from its garrison commander, Amano Kagehira, forced Ieyasu to abort the siege. During their retreat, Kagehira launched a counterattack to pursue Ieyasu, but this was repelled by [Mizuno Tadashige](/wiki/Mizuno_Tadashige "Mizuno Tadashige") and Torii Mototada, who led the rearguard.[[66]](#cite_note-74)

In April 1575, Ōga Yashirō, a deputy governor of over 20 villages in Oku district of Mikawa under Matsudaira Nobuyasu,[[67]](#cite_note-Hamada_Koichiro;_Yashiro_incident-75) was arrested by [Ōkubo Tadayo](/wiki/%C5%8Ckubo_Tadayo "Ōkubo Tadayo") and paraded around [Hamamatsu Castle](/wiki/Hamamatsu_Castle "Hamamatsu Castle"). According to the investigation, Yashirō was implicated in allegations of colluding with Takeda Katsuyori to betray Ieyasu and invade the Tokugawa clan's territory. According to a letter, Yashiro had teamed up with Takeda Katsuyori of Kai to seize Okazaki Castle. However, one of Yashiro's colleagues, Yamada Hachizō, betrayed Yashiro and passed this information to Nobuyasu. Meanwhile, Ieyasu himself also learned about Yashirō's further crimes of corruption in governance through a vassal's report.[[67]](#cite_note-Hamada_Koichiro;_Yashiro_incident-75) Yashirō was then executed by being mutilated alive with a saw, while Tadayo crucified his wife and children.[[68]](#cite_note-OgaYashiro;_Watanabe_Daimon1-76)

In June, during Takeda Katsuyori's raid on Mikawa Province when he [attacked Yoshida Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Yoshida_Castle "Siege of Yoshida Castle") and [besieged Nagashino Castle](/wiki/Battle_of_Nagashino "Battle of Nagashino"), Ieyasu appealed to Nobunaga for help; Nobunaga came personally with 30,000 men. The Oda-Tokugawa forces 38,000 strong won a great victory and successfully defended Nagashino Castle. Though the Takeda forces had been destroyed, Katsuyori survived the battle and retreated to [Kai Province](/wiki/Kai_Province "Kai Province").[[69]](#cite_note-77) For the next seven years, Ieyasu and Katsuyori fought a series of small battles through which Ieyasu's troops managed to wrest control of [Suruga Province](/wiki/Suruga_Province "Suruga Province") from the Takeda clan.

### Nobuyasu Incident

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Nobuyasu Incident")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/%E7%AF%89%E5%B1%B1%E6%AE%BF.jpg/250px-%E7%AF%89%E5%B1%B1%E6%AE%BF.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E7%AF%89%E5%B1%B1%E6%AE%BF.jpg)

Tsukiyama-Dono, wife of Ieyasu who was executed due to an accusation of treason

In 1579, [Lady Tsukiyama](/wiki/Lady_Tsukiyama "Lady Tsukiyama"), Ieyasu's wife, and his heir Matsudaira Nobuyasu were accused by Nobunaga of conspiring with Takeda Katsuyori to assassinate Nobunaga, whose daughter Tokuhime was married to Nobuyasu. Ieyasu ordered his wife to be executed and forced his son to commit *[seppuku](/wiki/Seppuku "Seppuku")* because of these accusations.[[70]](#cite_note-Ōoku_The_Secret_World_of_the_Shogun's_Women;_Tadatsugu_Sakai-78)[[71]](#cite_note-Shogun_The_Life_of_Tokugawa_Ieyasu;_Lady_Tsukiyama-79)

There are various theories regarding this incident. According to the "Mikawa Monogatari'", which was written by [Ōkubo Tadachika](/wiki/%C5%8Ckubo_Tadachika "Ōkubo Tadachika"), Tokuhime, wife of Nobuyasu, who was not on good terms with her mother-in-law Tsukiyama-dono, wrote in a letter to her father, Nobunaga, that her mother-in-law and her husband were secretly conspiring with Takeda Katsuyori.[[34]](#cite_note-Tetsuo_Owada_2002_24-40)

However, this hypothesis has been considered implausible by various historians in the modern era. According to Katsuhiro Taniguchi, the more plausible theory was that there was friction within the house of Tokugawa clan between two factions with conflicting ideals, as proposed by the writer Tenkyu Goro. One faction, dubbed the "Hamamatsu Castle Faction," was active on the front lines and had many opportunities to advance their careers. The other faction was the "Okazaki Castle Faction," which consisted of Tokugawa vassals responsible for logistical support due to past injuries and other factors that caused them to play a lesser role in the politics of Tokugawa clan. According to this theory, the conflict between these two factions eventually led to a conflict between Ieyasu, representing the Hamamatsu faction, and his son Nobuyasu, representing the Okazaki faction, finally ending with Nobuyasu's death in prison. Nobuyasu planned to exile Ieyasu with the help of the Okazaki Castle faction. Before and after his son's execution, Ieyasu punished or executed many of those who worked at Okazaki Castle, although some escaped. Taniguchi theorized that Tsukiyama also participated in the coup d'état that was going on in Okazaki Castle.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaniguchi2007209–211-80) Furthermore, Sakai Tadatsugu, the most prominent general of Ieyasu, also may have played a role in confirming Oda Nobunaga's suspicion of the alleged betrayal against the Oda clan being planned by Nobuyasu Tsukiyama. Ieyasu may have concluded that if a high-ranking [fudai daimyō](/wiki/Fudai_daimy%C5%8D "Fudai daimyō") such as Tadatsugu had confirmed the accusations against Lady Tsukiyama, then they must be true.[[70]](#cite_note-Ōoku_The_Secret_World_of_the_Shogun's_Women;_Tadatsugu_Sakai-78)

Another theory has said that Tadatsugu was actually conspiring with the Ieyasu's mother, [Odai no Kata](/wiki/Odai_no_Kata "Odai no Kata"), to get rid of Lady Tsukiyama.[[70]](#cite_note-Ōoku_The_Secret_World_of_the_Shogun's_Women;_Tadatsugu_Sakai-78) [Arthur Lindsay Sadler](/wiki/Arthur_Lindsay_Sadler "Arthur Lindsay Sadler") theorized this was a deliberate act of spite from Tadatsugu due to many senior Tokugawa clan generals' dislike of Nobuyasu.[[71]](#cite_note-Shogun_The_Life_of_Tokugawa_Ieyasu;_Lady_Tsukiyama-79)

In the same year, Ieyasu named his third son, [Tokugawa Hidetada](/wiki/Tokugawa_Hidetada "Tokugawa Hidetada"), as his heir, since his second son had been adopted by [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi"), who would later become a powerful daimyo.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Takeda clan annihilation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Takeda clan annihilation")]

Main articles: [Siege of Takatenjin (1581)](/wiki/Siege_of_Takatenjin_(1581) "Siege of Takatenjin (1581)") and [Battle of Tenmokuzan](/wiki/Battle_of_Tenmokuzan "Battle of Tenmokuzan")

[![Map](https://maps.wikimedia.org/img/osm-intl,11,34.698304,138.035563,300x250.png?lang=en&domain=en.wikipedia.org&title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&revid=1343475946&groups=_baccdd7623398b327cff0f20b69fcbb269b64092&parser=legacy)](/wiki/Special:Map/11/34.698304/138.035563/en)

Positions of the Takatenjin Castle and the six forts  
1.*Ogasayama Fort,* 2.*Nogasaka Fort*, 3.*Higamine Fort,*4. *Shishigahana Fort*, 5.*Nakamura Fort*, 6.'*Mitsuiyama Fort*, 7.*Takatenjin Castle*

In 1580, Ieyasu built five fortresses in order to isolate Takatenjin Castle from external supplies and reinforcements.[[73]](#cite_note-kakegawa20171006-81)[[74]](#cite_note-mizuno20150920-82)[[75]](#cite_note-mizuno20150927-83) In addition to those five new fortresses, Ieyasu renovated an old castle named Ogasayama fort, which had originally been built by Ieyasu far before the conquest of Tōtōmi Province against Imagawa clan to capture [Kakegawa Castle](/wiki/Kakegawa_Castle "Kakegawa Castle").[[75]](#cite_note-mizuno20150927-83) With the six fortresses, which were referred to as the "six fortresses of Takatenjin", completed, Ieyasu assigned Ishikawa Yasumichi to the Ogasayama fort, [Honda Yasushige](/wiki/Honda_Yasushige_(1554-1611) "Honda Yasushige (1554-1611)") to the Nogasaka fort, Osuga Yasutaka to the Higamine,[[73]](#cite_note-kakegawa20171006-81) Shishigahana,[[76]](#cite_note-84) and Nakamura forts,[[73]](#cite_note-kakegawa20171006-81) while Sakai Ietada was appointed to garrison the Mitsuiyama fort.[[74]](#cite_note-mizuno20150920-82)[[73]](#cite_note-kakegawa20171006-81)

The Takatenjin castle which was defended by [Okabe Motonobu](/wiki/Okabe_Motonobu "Okabe Motonobu"), immediately suffered from a period of starvation as the siege by Oda-Tokugawa forces intensified.[[73]](#cite_note-kakegawa20171006-81) In response, Motonobu tried to negotiate a truce with Ieyasu by offering Takisakai and Koyama Castle in exchange for Takatenjin castle being spared from the siege.[[77]](#cite_note-85) After Ieyasu consulted with Nobunaga on this matter, he refused Motonobu's plea. Nobunaga stated his reason in a letter saying that if Takeda Katsuyori sent his forces to assist Motonobu, then there would be an opportunity to bait the Takeda army into coming outside the castle and annihilate them on the field. Meanwhile, Nobunaga also stated that if Katsuyori neglected helping Motonobu at all, it would damage the Takeda clan's credibility because they could not save their own vassals.[[78]](#cite_note-86)

In 1581, Ieyasu forces managed to subdue Tanaka castle,[[79]](#cite_note-Tadakatsu;Kajiwara_Ai-87)[[80]](#cite_note-88)[[81]](#cite_note-89)[[82]](#cite_note-90) and [recapture Takatenjin castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Takatenjin_(1581) "Siege of Takatenjin (1581)"), where Okabe Motonobu was killed during the fight.[[83]](#cite_note-kotobank-91)

The end of the war with Takeda Katsuyori came in 1582, when a combined Oda-Tokugawa force attacked and conquered [Kai Province](/wiki/Kai_Province "Kai Province"), where Katsuyori was cornered and defeated at the [Battle of Tenmokuzan](/wiki/Battle_of_Tenmokuzan "Battle of Tenmokuzan"), and then committed *seppuku*.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurnbull1998231-92) With the Takeda clan practically annihilated as political entity, Nobunaga gave Ieyasu the right to govern [Suruga Province](/wiki/Suruga_Province "Suruga Province") in recognition of his service in the fight against the Takeda clan.[[85]](#cite_note-Susumu_Shimazaki_Suruga-93)

## Tokugawa expansions (1582–1584)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Tokugawa expansions (1582–1584)")]

Main article: [Honnō-ji Incident](/wiki/Honn%C5%8D-ji_Incident "Honnō-ji Incident")

In late June 1582, before the incident at [Honnō-ji temple](/wiki/Honn%C5%8D-ji_Incident "Honnō-ji Incident"), [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") invited Ieyasu to tour the [Kansai region](/wiki/Kansai_region "Kansai region") in celebration of the demise of the [Takeda clan](/wiki/Takeda_clan "Takeda clan"). Before the meeting could take place, Ieyasu learned that Nobunaga had killed himself at Honnō-ji temple after being surrounded and overwhelmed by the forces of [Akechi Mitsuhide](/wiki/Akechi_Mitsuhide "Akechi Mitsuhide").

### "Shinkun Iga-goe" journey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: \"Shinkun Iga-goe\" journey")]

When Ieyasu heard that Nobunaga had been killed by Akechi Mitsuhide he had only a few companions with him,[[86]](#cite_note-THE_NINJA_BOOK:_The_New_Mansenshukai-94) notably Sakai Tadatsugu, Ii Naomasa, Honda Tadakatsu, [Sakakibara Yasumasa](/wiki/Sakakibara_Yasumasa "Sakakibara Yasumasa") and some others.[[47]](#cite_note-Sansom_1961-54): 314–315[[87]](#cite_note-SAMURAIHUNTER2-95)[[88]](#cite_note-96) The Iga provincial route was dangerous because of the *[Ochimusha-gari](/wiki/Ochimusha "Ochimusha")*, or "Samurai hunters" gangs.[[i]](#cite_note-99) Ieyasu and his party, therefore, chose the shortest route back to Mikawa Province by crossing [Iga Province](/wiki/Iga_Province "Iga Province"). The exact route differs in many versions according to primary sources *Tokugawa Nikki* or *Mikawa Todai-Hon*:

* The *Tokugawa Nikki* theory stated that Ieyasu took the roads to Shijonawate and Son'enji, then followed the Kizu River until they spent a night in the Yamaguchi Castle. The next day they reached a stronghold of the [Kōka ikki](/wiki/K%C5%8Dka_ikki "Kōka ikki") clan branch from Tarao who allowed them to take refuge for the night. On the last day, Ieyasu's group used a ship from Shiroko to reach [Okazaki Castle](/wiki/Okazaki_Castle "Okazaki Castle").[[86]](#cite_note-THE_NINJA_BOOK:_The_New_Mansenshukai-94) However, The *Tokugawa Nikki* theory is doubted by modern historians, since it was not the shortest route for Ieyasu to reach Mikawa from his starting position in [Sakai](/wiki/Sakai "Sakai"),[[91]](#cite_note-IetadaNikkiIgacross-100) and it was considered by historical researchers to be a very risky path due to the existence of [Iga ikki](/wiki/Iga_ikki "Iga ikki") clans which were hostile to the Oda and Tokugawa clans.[[92]](#cite_note-Igacrossing1-101)[[93]](#cite_note-fujita-102)
* The *Mikawa Toda-Hon* theory stated that Ieyasu went north from Ogawadate, crossed Koka, and entered Seishu Seki (from Shigaraki), passing through Aburahi and entering Tsuge in Iga.[[93]](#cite_note-fujita-102) This theory was championed by modern Japanese historians such as Tatsuo Fujita from [Mie University](/wiki/Mie_University "Mie University"), who took this material to formulate three different theories about the details of Ieyasu's trek.[[94]](#cite_note-103)[[93]](#cite_note-fujita-102) This theory is also supported by a group of historical researchers from Mie city, who happened to be descendants of the [Kōka ikki](/wiki/K%C5%8Dka_ikki "Kōka ikki") clans. The researchers stated that by taking this path, before Ieyasu's group reached Kada pass where they could be escorted by the Kōka clan [Jizamurai](/wiki/Jizamurai "Jizamurai"), Ieyasu mostly depended on the protection of his high-rank vassals, particularly the four [Shitennō](/wiki/Shitenn%C5%8D_(Tokugawa_clan) "Shitennō (Tokugawa clan)") generals of the Tokugawa clan, rather than the popular theory about getting help from the "Iga Ninja" clans.[[92]](#cite_note-Igacrossing1-101)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/%E5%9B%BD%E9%81%9325%E5%8F%B7.jpg/250px-%E5%9B%BD%E9%81%9325%E5%8F%B7.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%9B%BD%E9%81%9325%E5%8F%B7.jpg)

Kada pass, believed to be the road which taken by Tokugawa Ieyasu to return into Mikawa province.[[93]](#cite_note-fujita-102)

Regardless which theory was true, historians agreed that when his path ended at Kada (a mountain pass between [Kameyama](/wiki/Kameyama,_Mie "Kameyama, Mie") and Iga), the Tokugawa group suffered one last attack by Ochimusha-gari outlaws as they reached the territory of [Kōka ikki](/wiki/K%C5%8Dka_ikki "Kōka ikki") clans of [Jizamurai](/wiki/Jizamurai "Jizamurai") who were friendly to the Tokugawa clan. The Koka ikki samurais assisted Ieyasu in eliminating the threat of the Ochimusha-gari outlaws and escorted them until they reached [Iga Province](/wiki/Iga_Province "Iga Province"), where they were further protected by other allied clans from Iga ikki who accompanied Ieyasu and his group until they safely reached Mikawa.[[90]](#cite_note-SAMURAIHUNTER3-98)

Portuguese missionary [Luís Fróis](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Fr%C3%B3is "Luís Fróis") recorded in his work *History of Japan* that during this journey, Tokugawa retainers including [Sakai Tadatsugu](/wiki/Sakai_Tadatsugu "Sakai Tadatsugu"), [Ii Naomasa](/wiki/Ii_Naomasa "Ii Naomasa") and [Honda Tadakatsu](/wiki/Honda_Tadakatsu "Honda Tadakatsu") fought their way out of raids and harassment from Ochimusha-gari outlaws while escorting Ieyasu, while paying bribes of gold and silver to those Ochimusha-gari outlaws that could be bribed.[[95]](#cite_note-SAMURAIHUNTER4-104) [Matsudaira Ietada](/wiki/Matsudaira_Ietada_(Fuk%C5%8Dzu) "Matsudaira Ietada (Fukōzu)") recorded in his journal, *Ietada nikki* (家忠日記), the Ieyasu's escorts suffered around 200 casualties during their journey, and when they arrived at Ietada's residence in Mikawa, they only had about 34 personnel left, including high-ranking Tokugawa generals including Tadatsugu, Naomasa, Tadakatsu, Sakakibara, [Ōkubo Tadayo](/wiki/%C5%8Ckubo_Tadayo "Ōkubo Tadayo"), [Hattori Hanzō](/wiki/Hattori_Hanz%C5%8D "Hattori Hanzō"), and others.[[96]](#cite_note-105)[[97]](#cite_note-106)

### Tenshō-Jingo war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Tenshō-Jingo war")]

Further information: [Tenshō-Jingo war](/wiki/Tensh%C5%8D-Jingo_war "Tenshō-Jingo war")

After Ieyasu reached Mikawa, he immediately moved to shift his focus to former Takeda clan territory as he expected unrest there. As a preemptive measure, Ieyasu dispatched Honda Nobutoshi to contact [Kawajiri Hidetaka](/wiki/Kawajiri_Hidetaka "Kawajiri Hidetaka"), who ruled [Suwa District](/wiki/Suwa_District,_Nagano "Suwa District, Nagano") as a vassal of the Oda clan, to request cooperation.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama2015127-107)

Meanwhile, Ieyasu had Yoda Nobushige, the former Takeda clan governor of the Saku district who had been hidden in the Tokugawa territory and had maintained contact with Ieyasu, organize contacts with the other remaining vassals of the Takeda clan to restore order in the chaos following the death of Nobunaga.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama2015127-107) At that moment, Nobushige was settled in the secluded village of Osawa.[[99]](#cite_note-108) At the same time, Uesugi and the Hōjō clans also mobilized their forces to invade [Shinano Province](/wiki/Shinano_Province "Shinano Province"), [Kōzuke Province](/wiki/K%C5%8Dzuke_Province "Kōzuke Province"), and [Kai Province](/wiki/Kai_Province "Kai Province") (currently Gunma Prefecture), which were ruled by the remnants of the many small clans that formerly served the Takeda clan, when they learned of the death of Nobunaga. This caused a triangular conflict between those three factions known as the **[Tenshō-Jingo War](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A9%E6%AD%A3%E5%A3%AC%E5%8D%88%E3%81%AE%E4%B9%B1 "jp:天正壬午の乱")** (天正壬午の乱, *Tenshō-Jingo no ran*).[[100]](#cite_note-Tensho_Jingo_war-109)[[j]](#cite_note-112)[[103]](#cite_note-113)[[104]](#cite_note-114)[[105]](#cite_note-115)[[106]](#cite_note-116)

#### Preliminary movements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Preliminary movements")]

At first, the Hōjō clan, who ruled the Kantō region, led an army of 55,000 men to invade the Shinano Province through Usui,[[100]](#cite_note-Tensho_Jingo_war-109) as they aimed to prevent a Tokugawa incursion of Kai.[[107]](#cite_note-Susumu_Shimazaki;_Tensho_Jingo_war-117) By June 13, 1582, the Hōjō clan had captured Iwadono Castle in Tsuru District and instructed Watanabe Shozaemon, a local magistrate from the Tsuru District, to assist them in their conquest.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama2015132–133-118) Subsequently, [Sanada Masayuki](/wiki/Sanada_Masayuki "Sanada Masayuki") led his army and captured the [Numata Castle](/wiki/Numata_Castle "Numata Castle") for the Uesugi clan.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama201591–97-119)

Meanwhile, Ieyasu immediately marched his 8,000 soldiers to the disputed regions[[100]](#cite_note-Tensho_Jingo_war-109) and then split his army into two parts, with the separate detachment led by Sakai Tadatsugu and Ogasawara Nobumine going to pacify the Shinano Province, while Ieyasu took the main force to pacify Kai. Tadatsugu and Nobumine met with unexpected resistance from Suwa Yoritada, a former Takeda vassal who was now allied with the Hōjō clan.[[110]](#cite_note-120) They were beaten by Yoritada, who were then reinforced by the Hōjō clan, forcing Tadatsugu to retreat. As Tadatsugu's forces retreated, they were pursued by 43,000 men of the Hōjō clan army. Okabe Masatsuna, a member of the Suruga clan samurai who once served under [Baba Nobuharu](/wiki/Baba_Nobuharu "Baba Nobuharu"),[[111]](#cite_note-rekishikaido;_Tensho-Jingo1-121) took the initiative to defend the rear of Tadatsugu's army from the enemy charges and repelled them.[[111]](#cite_note-rekishikaido;_Tensho-Jingo1-121) As they successfully retreated without further losses, they rejoined Ieyasu's main forces in the area of Wakamiko in Suwamachi (modern day [Yamanashi Prefecture](/wiki/Yamanashi_Prefecture "Yamanashi Prefecture")).[[112]](#cite_note-wakamikobattle-122)

On June 5, Ieyasu instructed Orii Tsugumasa and Yonekura Tadatsugu, two members of Takekawa clan from Kai who were hiding in Kiriyama, Tōtōmi Province, to proceed with the work of enticing the Kai samurai to the Tokugawa side. The next day, Ieyasu also sent a letter to Masatsuna instructing him to begin the construction of a castle at Shimoyama, Minobu Town in the Kai Kawachi domain, the former base of [Anayama Nobutada](/wiki/Anayama_Nobutada "Anayama Nobutada"), one of Tokugawa's retainers who was killed by outlaws during his escape after the Honnō-ji Incident.[[90]](#cite_note-SAMURAIHUNTER3-98) Suganuma Castle (Terazawa, Minobu Town) was built along the Fuji River and the Suruga Highway (Kawachi Road). After the death of Nobutada and the senior members of the Obikane clan, to which they had pledged loyalty, the Anayama clan was left leaderless, so they decided to pledge allegiance to Ieyasu.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama2015126–127-123)

Ieyasu then dispatched [Sone Masatada](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%9B%BD%E6%A0%B9%E6%98%8C%E4%B8%96 "jp:曽根昌世"), formerly one of Shingen Takeda's three most prominent generals,[[k]](#cite_note-124) to the Erinji area with 500 men to confront a Hōjō force that numbered 3,000 men. Masatada managed to defeat them and inflicted between 600 and 700 casualties. On June 12, Masatada joined forces with another former Takeda vassal, [Okabe Masatsuna](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%B2%A1%E9%83%A8%E6%AD%A3%E7%B6%B1 "jp:岡部正綱"),[[111]](#cite_note-rekishikaido;_Tensho-Jingo1-121) and a senior Tokugawa general, Osuga Yasutaka.[[114]](#cite_note-125) The same day, Yoda Nobushige set off to Saku District and rallied around 3,000 Takeda clan retainers as Ieyasu instructed.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama2015128-126)

Later on June 28, Ieyasu also dispatched the Anayama clan to resist the Hōjō clan. He also sent his generals [Ōkubo Tadayo](/wiki/%C5%8Ckubo_Tadayo "Ōkubo Tadayo"), Ishikawa Yasumichi and Honda Hirotaka along with his son Yasushige as reinforcements for them to resist the Hōjō.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama2015132–133-118)

At some point during this war, the Tokugawa clan also gained support from the [Tomohisa clan](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%9F%A5%E4%B9%85%E6%B0%8F "jp:知久氏").[[l]](#cite_note-128)

[Uesugi Kagekatsu](/wiki/Uesugi_Kagekatsu "Uesugi Kagekatsu") of the Uesugi clan also made his move by supporting former Takeda clan forces under the leadership of Ogasawara Dōsetsusai from the [Ogasawara clan](/wiki/Ogasawara_clan "Ogasawara clan") and Yashiro Hidemasa at [Chikuma](/wiki/Chikuma,_Nagano "Chikuma, Nagano") and the Nishina clan of [Azumino](/wiki/Azumino "Azumino"). They defeated and expelled [Kiso Yoshimasa](/wiki/Kiso_Yoshimasa "Kiso Yoshimasa"), who had been granted the control of both Chikuma and Azumino by Oda Nobunaga.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama2015167-129) They then faced another branch of Ogasawara clan which was led by Ogasawara Sadayoshi and his retainers which opposed the steps taken by Dōsetsusai. Sadayoshi's group appealed to the Tokugawa clan and offered their allegiance to Ieyasu.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama2015188-130) On June 24, Kagekatsu advanced into northern Shinano and entered [Naganuma castle](/wiki/Naganuma_castle "Naganuma castle").[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShinichi200533-131)

As the triangular battle was underway between the three factions, order was restored in [Owari province](/wiki/Owari_Province "Owari Province") as the rebellion of Akechi Mitsuhide had already been suppressed in the [Battle of Yamazaki](/wiki/Battle_of_Yamazaki "Battle of Yamazaki"). Ieyasu also informed the Oda clan of the developments in Shinano and Kai.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama201582,_124,_126-132) In response, on July 7, as the Oda clan learned of the defeat of [Takigawa Kazumasu](/wiki/Takigawa_Kazumasu "Takigawa Kazumasu") by the Hōjō clan at the [Battle of Kanagawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Kanagawa "Battle of Kanagawa"), [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") sent a letter to Ieyasu to give him authorization to lead military operations to secure the two provinces of Kai and Shinano from the Hōjō and Uesugi clans.[[m]](#cite_note-134)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Komoro_castle00s3872.jpg/250px-Komoro_castle00s3872.jpg)](/wiki/File:Komoro_castle00s3872.jpg)

Komoro castle

### Aftermath of Tenshō-Jingo war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Aftermath of Tenshō-Jingo war")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Bansho-in_%28Saku%29.JPG/250px-Bansho-in_%28Saku%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bansho-in_(Saku).JPG)

Grave of [Yoda Nobushige](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%BE%9D%E7%94%B0%E4%BF%A1%E8%95%83 "jp:依田信蕃"), former Takeda clan general and benefactor of Ieyasu during the Tenshō-Jingo War.[[122]](#cite_note-Yoda_Nobushige_Wikisource-135)

The problems for the Hōjō clan increased by the day as Ieyasu established contact with daimyo lords from north-east Japan including the [Satake](/wiki/Satake_clan "Satake clan"), [Yuki](/wiki/Y%C5%ABki_clan "Yūki clan"), and Utsunomiya clans, who threatened to invade the Hōjō from behind while the Hōjō were still engaging Ieyasu in battle.[[107]](#cite_note-Susumu_Shimazaki;_Tensho_Jingo_war-117) As the war turned in favor of Ieyasu, combined with the defection of Sanada Masayuki to the Tokugawa side, the Hōjō negotiated a truce with Ieyasu.[[123]](#cite_note-136)

The Hōjō clan sent Hōjō Ujinobu as representative, while the Tokugawa sent Ii Naomasa as representative for the preliminary meetings.[[124]](#cite_note-137)[[125]](#cite_note-138) Representatives from the Oda clan, [Oda Nobukatsu](/wiki/Oda_Nobukatsu "Oda Nobukatsu"), [Oda Nobutaka](/wiki/Oda_Nobutaka "Oda Nobutaka"), and Toyotomi Hideyoshi who mediated the negotiation from October 1582, also assisted the ratification of the truce.[[126]](#cite_note-139) Sanada Nobutada, a younger brother of Sanada Masayuki, was given 5,000 [koku](/wiki/Koku "Koku") of territory by Ieyasu,[[127]](#cite_note-140) and Okabe Masatsuna was rewarded with a 7,600 koku domain in between Kai and Shinano provinces.[[111]](#cite_note-rekishikaido;_Tensho-Jingo1-121) In the aftermath of the war, Ieyasu once again sent Tadatsugu to subdue Suwa Yoritada at [Suwa](/wiki/Suwa,_Nagano "Suwa, Nagano") in Shinano in December, where Tadatsugu defeated Yoritada and secured his surrender to the Tokugawa clan.[[128]](#cite_note-tenshojingoAbeNishimura-141)

In March 1583, according to the [Meishō genkō-roku](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%90%8D%E5%B0%86%E8%A8%80%E8%A1%8C%E9%8C%B2 "jp:名将言行録") record, after the destruction of the Takeda clan in the Tenmokuzan, Ieyasu [organized a kishōmon (blood oath)](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A9%E6%AD%A3%E5%A3%AC%E5%8D%88%E8%B5%B7%E8%AB%8B%E6%96%87 "jp:天正壬午起請文") with many samurai clans, local lords, low rank officials, ninja mercenaries, and even noble ladies that were formerly vassals of the Takeda clan in order to put them under the command of Tokugawa clan retainers.[[129]](#cite_note-142) Since the ritual took place after the Tenshō-Jingo war and in the same location, this oath taking ritual was named *Tenshō-Jingo kishōmon*.[[130]](#cite_note-143) During the process of the oath-taking Tokugawa, Ieyasu planned to give control of most of the former Takeda samurai to Ii Naomasa to command, having consulted and reached agreement with Sakai Tadatsugu, a senior Tokugawa clan vassal. However, Ieyasu's decision garnered protest from Sakakibara Yasumasa, who went so far as to threaten Naomasa. Tadatsugu immediately defended Ieyasu's decision in response and warned Yasumasa that if he did any harm to Naomasa, Tadatsugu would personally slaughter the [Sakakibara clan](/wiki/Sakakibara_clan "Sakakibara clan"); Yasumasa backed down and did not protest further.[[131]](#cite_note-Meisho_Genkoroku-144) As there were no more protests, Ieyasu decided to assign the new recruits to various commands, as following:[[132]](#cite_note-Tsuchiya_clan;_Naomasa-145)[[133]](#cite_note-146)[[134]](#cite_note-147)[[135]](#cite_note-148)

* 70 former Takeda samurai from [Tsuchiya clan](/wiki/Tsuchiya_clan "Tsuchiya clan"), and also the clan of Ishiguro Shōgen to Ii Naomasa.[[132]](#cite_note-Tsuchiya_clan;_Naomasa-145) (another source mentioned that total of 120 Takeda samurai warriors came under the command of Naomasa.[[136]](#cite_note-hikae-149))
* 11 former Takeda samurai from Komai clan led by [Komai Masanao](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E9%A7%92%E4%BA%95%E6%94%BF%E7%9B%B4 "jp:駒井政直") to Sakakibara Yasumasa.
* 60 former Takeda samurai of Asari clan led by Asari Masatane to Honda Tadakatsu.
* The largest number of Takeda clans vassals were under the direct control of Ieyasu himself, including clans which were led by Yoda Nobushige or Hoshina Masanao (along with the local daimyo lords from Shinano who followed Masanao).,[[137]](#cite_note-mochizuki-150) the Kurihara to Kurihara Nobumori,[[138]](#cite_note-151) 49 samurai from the Jō clan led by father and son Jō Kageshige and Jō Masashige,[[139]](#cite_note-takeda-152) samurai who formerly guarded the frontiers of Takeda clan led Watanabe Hitoyanosuke,[[140]](#cite_note-153) and many others. Among those who were assigned as [Hatamoto](/wiki/Hatamoto "Hatamoto"), or direct vassal of Ieyasu, they were allowed to retain their positions, and even increased the revenue from the domains they controlled, particularly from the new territories which the Tokugawa clan conquered. This was apparent from the Saegusa clan, where the son of the clan leader, Saegusa Masayoshi, retained his territory,[[141]](#cite_note-154) while his father Saegusa Torayoshi was appointed as one of four magistrates in the Tokugawa clan.[[142]](#cite_note-155)

Aside from the already established workforces from the former Takeda clan, Ieyasu also established new offices such as the **Hachiōji sen'nin-dōshin**, which formed from a patchwork membership of nine small clans of Takeda retainers. This group continued to serve the Tokugawa clan faithfully until they were disbanded during the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") in 1868.[[n]](#cite_note-157)

In 1583, Ieyasu had a detachment of Ii Naomasa's troops conquer the Takatō area of Shinano, which had still not submitted to the Tokugawa clan.[[144]](#cite_note-hiroyuki_kikuchi;_Ii_Naomasa-158) Meanwhile, Nobushige led the attack against the [Tomono clan](/wiki/Tomono_clan "Tomono clan"), and defeated them.[[145]](#cite_note-159)

In the middle of operation, Yoda Nobushige was killed in action. Yoda Yasukuni, who succeeded him as head of the Yoda clan, was given the surname Matsudaira and Komoro Castle. The territory he was allowed to inherit was 60,000 koku, one of the largest for any of Ieyasu's vassals at the time.[[146]](#cite_note-160) Yashiro Hidemasa, who surrendered to Ogasawara Sadayoshi, also joined the Tokugawa clan later on April 1, 1584, together with his younger brother Ogasawara Mitsutoshi.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama2011128-161)[[148]](#cite_note-162)

Ieyasu did not take a side during the [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") and [Shibata Katsuie](/wiki/Shibata_Katsuie "Shibata Katsuie") conflict, where Hideyoshi defeated Katsuie at the [Battle of Shizugatake](/wiki/Battle_of_Shizugatake "Battle of Shizugatake").[[47]](#cite_note-Sansom_1961-54): 314

## Ieyasu and Hideyoshi (1584–1598)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Ieyasu and Hideyoshi (1584–1598)")]

### Earthquakes and conflict with Hideyoshi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Earthquakes and conflict with Hideyoshi")]

See also: [Battle of Komaki and Nagakute](/wiki/Battle_of_Komaki_and_Nagakute "Battle of Komaki and Nagakute")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Nagakute_Historic_Battlefield.jpg/250px-Nagakute_Historic_Battlefield.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagakute_Historic_Battlefield.jpg)

Nagakute Historic Battlefield located in [Aichi Prefecture](/wiki/Aichi_Prefecture "Aichi Prefecture")

As the Tokugawa clan's territory expanded, many of their regions were hit by earthquakes and heavy rains from 1583 to 1584. In particular, from May to July, heavy rains fell constantly from the Kantō region to the [Tōkai region](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkai_region "Tōkai region"), in what was dubbed the "heaviest flood in 50 years" in the historical record of *Ietada-nikki*.[[149]](#cite_note-163) It was under these circumstances that the Tokugawa clan was forced to fight against the Hōjō clan and the Toyotomi government, because in 1584 Ieyasu had decided to support [Oda Nobukatsu](/wiki/Oda_Nobukatsu "Oda Nobukatsu"), the eldest surviving son and heir of Oda Nobunaga, against [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi"). The *Ryumonji Kojiki*, written by successive chief priests at Ryumonji Temple in Tahara, Mikawa Province, records that in 1582 many people were mobilized in the Battle of Komaki and Nagakute, which led to the devastation of farmland and famine.

The devastation of the Tokugawa clan's territory made it difficult to continue fighting against the Toyotomi government and they were forced to rebuild their country.[[150]](#cite_note-164) This was a dangerous situation for the Tokugawa clan which could have resulted in their annihilation due to the Oda clan collapsing after Nobunaga's death.[[151]](#cite_note-The_Maker_of_Modern_Japan_The_Life_of_Tokugawa_Ieyasu;_Yasumasa_proclamation-165)

Tokugawa troops took the traditional Oda stronghold of [Owari](/wiki/Owari_Province "Owari Province"). Hideyoshi responded by sending an army into Owari. Ieyasu decided to confront Hideyoshi's forces in [Komaki](/wiki/Komaki "Komaki") because his general, Sakakibara Yasumasa, suggested the area was favorable for the Tokugawa force to fight incoming enemies from the west.[[152]](#cite_note-The_Samurai_A_Military_History;_Komaki_Nagakute-166) Additionally, Ieyasu and Nobukatsu formed an anti-Hideyoshi alliance with [Chōsokabe Motochika](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dsokabe_Motochika "Chōsokabe Motochika"), through contact with [Kōsokabe Chikayasu](/wiki/K%C5%8Dsokabe_Chikayasu "Kōsokabe Chikayasu").[[153]](#cite_note-167)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Hoshizaki_Castle_001.JPG/250px-Hoshizaki_Castle_001.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hoshizaki_Castle_001.JPG)

Stone memorial stele on the former site with brief description of the history of Hoshizaki castle (2009)

At first, Ieyasu ordered his generals [Mizuno Tadashige](/wiki/Mizuno_Tadashige "Mizuno Tadashige") and [Mizuno Katsunari](/wiki/Mizuno_Katsushige "Mizuno Katsushige") to capture [Hoshizaki Castle](/wiki/Hoshizaki_Castle "Hoshizaki Castle").[[154]](#cite_note-Funahashi;NagakuteKatsunari-168) Then, as the Toyotomi army vanguard under the command of [Toyotomi Hidetsugu](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidetsugu "Toyotomi Hidetsugu") started entering the area of Komaki, Ieyasu sent Yasumasa and Osuga Yasutaka in for a surprise attack and inflicted heavy losses on the Hidetsugu army before they were stopped by [Hori Hidemasa](/wiki/Hori_Hidemasa "Hori Hidemasa"). Hidemasa decided to retreat when Ieyasu brought his main forces forward.[[155]](#cite_note-169)

Later, as another Toyotomi vanguard led by [Mori Nagayoshi](/wiki/Mori_Nagayoshi "Mori Nagayoshi") entered the area, Ieyasu had his senior generals, Sakai Tadatsugu, [Okudaira Nobumasa](/wiki/Okudaira_Nobumasa "Okudaira Nobumasa"), and Matsudaira Ietada, beat Nagayoshi's troops, forcing him to retreat.[[156]](#cite_note-Toyotomi_Hideyoshi;_Stephen_Turnbull,_Komaki-Nagakute-170) In the last phase of this series of engagements, Nagayoshi returned with another Toyotomi general [Ikeda Tsuneoki](/wiki/Ikeda_Tsuneoki "Ikeda Tsuneoki"). However, both of them were repeatedly beaten on the field at Nagakute by Mizuno Katsunari,[[157]](#cite_note-Kusudo_Yoshiaki_2009_14-171) and later, Ii Naomasa, caused the Toyotomi forces to suffer heavy losses with both Tsuneoki and Nagayoshi killed in action.[[158]](#cite_note-Japanese_War_History_Volume_13:_Komaki_Role_1942_vers.-172)[[159]](#cite_note-Japanese_War_History_Volume_13:_Komaki_Role-173)

Furious with the decimation of Nagayoshi's and Tsuneoki's forces, Hideyoshi mobilized his main army to crush Ieyasu's army in Nagakute. However, Ieyasu had already retreated before Hideyoshi's main forces arrived.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStephen_Turnbull201227-174)[[161]](#cite_note-Mikawa_Go_Fudoki_Seisetsu_Daizen-175)

After the Battle of Komaki and Nagakute in April, the front line in northern Owari reached a stalemate. At this time, Kanie Castle was located about three miles between Ieyasu's Kiyosu Castle and Nobuo's Nagashima Castle, and was connected to the Mie moat and three castles: Ono Castle, Shimojima Castle, and Maeda Castle. At that time, Kanie Castle was facing the sea and was one of the leading ports in Owari, along with Atsuta and Tsushima. Then on June 18, Ieyasu and Nobuo led 20,000 soldiers and besieged three castles: Kanie Castle, Maeda Castle, and Shimojima Castle.[[162]](#cite_note-Komaki_Nagakute;_Tatsuo-176) Kanie Castle was defended by [Maeda Nagatane](/wiki/Maeda_Nagatane "Maeda Nagatane") and [Takigawa Kazumasu](/wiki/Takigawa_Kazumasu "Takigawa Kazumasu"). Tadatsugu, Okanabe Mori, and Yamaguchi Shigemasa spearheaded the attack towards Shimojima castle, while Sakakibara Yasumasa, Osuga Yasutaka were deployed to capture any fleeing defenders.[[163]](#cite_note-177)[[164]](#cite_note-178)

During this siege, Ieyasu's [hatamoto](/wiki/Hatamoto "Hatamoto") retainers, including Mizuno Katsunari, blockaded the port of the castle and hijacked two ships belonging to [Kuki Yoshitaka](/wiki/Kuki_Yoshitaka "Kuki Yoshitaka") to prevent any outside help for Kanie Castle.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirai199252-179) After the fall of Shimojima castle on June 22, Oda Nobuo and Tokugawa Ieyasu launched an all-out attack on Kanie Castle. The soldiers led by Tadatsugu, who had been deployed at the main gate, were exhausted after days of fierce fighting, and in the evening, the soldiers of Yasumasa Sakakibara and Ietada Matsudaira entered Kaimonjiguchi in their place.[[166]](#cite_note-180) On June 23, Ieyasu entered the castle with Sakakibara Yasumasa, subduing the castle.[[162]](#cite_note-Komaki_Nagakute;_Tatsuo-176)

### Becoming a vassal of the Toyotomi Regency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Becoming a vassal of the Toyotomi Regency")]

The conflict with Hideyoshi was long, and lasted until Nobukatsu decided to surrender to Hideyoshi. With this, Ieyasu lost his motivation to further oppose Hideyoshi and decided to also submit.[[167]](#cite_note-Sansom-181)[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurnbull1998235-182) After peace negotiations between Tokugawa Ieyasu and Toyotomi Hideyoshi in the aftermath of the [Battle of Komaki and Nagakute](/wiki/Battle_of_Komaki_and_Nagakute "Battle of Komaki and Nagakute"), Naomasa, Tadakatsu, and Yasumasa gained fame in Kyoto. The following month, the three of them were joined by Tadatsugu Sakai while accompanying Ieyasu on a trip to Kyoto, where the four of them became famous.[[169]](#cite_note-徳川四天王-183)

However, on November 13, 1585, [Ishikawa Kazumasa](/wiki/Ishikawa_Kazumasa "Ishikawa Kazumasa") defected from Ieyasu to Hideyoshi.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETanaka200714-184) Ogasawara Sadayoshi, who also defected and followed Kazumasa, led over 3,000 troops to attack Takato. In Takato Castle there were only 40 cavalrymen and 360 soldiers other than the elderly Hoshina Masatoshi, but Masatoshi himself took command and defeated the Ogasawara forces in open battle. This prevented the collapse of Tokugawa rule in Shinano and Ieyasu awarded Masanao with the sword of Tsunehisa on December 24 in recognition of his military achievements.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama2011179-182-185)

These incidents caused Ieyasu to undertake massive reforms of the Tokugawa clan governmental structure by incorporating more Takeda clan vassals into his administrations, both civil and military. At first, Ieyasu ordered Torii Mototada, who served as the county magistrate of Kai, to collect military laws, weapons, and military equipment from the time of Takeda Shingen and bring them to Hamamatsu Castle in Hamamatsu City, Shizuoka Prefecture. Later, he appointed two former Takeda vassals, Naruse Masakazu and Okabe Masatsuna, as magistrates under authority of Ii Naomasa and Honda Tadakatsu. He ordered all former Takeda vassals who served him to impart any military doctrines and structures they knew from their service to the Takeda clan.,[[172]](#cite_note-Shogun_and_Samurai_Tales_of_Nobunaga,_Hideyoshi,_and_Ieyasu-186) He ordered three of his prime generals, Ii Naomasa, Honda Tadakatsu, and Sakakibara Yasumasa, to serve as the supreme commanders of these new military regiments.[[173]](#cite_note-187) The same year, Hideyoshi forced his younger sister [Asahi no kata](/wiki/Asahi_no_kata "Asahi no kata") to divorce her husband, who then committed suicide, and sent her to Ieyasu with the offer of marrying her.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFujino199059-188)

In 1586, in response to Ishikawa Kazumasa's defection from the Tokugawa clan, former Takeda clan vassals from Kai and Shinano province, including Yonekura Tadatsugu, sought to reaffirm their loyalty to Ieyasu by presenting their family members as hostages.[[175]](#cite_note-189)

Later the same year, Hideyoshi sent [his own mother](/wiki/%C5%8Cmandokoro "Ōmandokoro") as a hostage to Ieyasu. If Ieyasu continued to refuse to go to Kyoto after such a gesture, it would give Hideyoshi a just cause for war. Ieyasu finally decided to become Hideyoshi's vassal.[[176]](#cite_note-190)

### Ruling Kantō and suppressing rebellions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Ruling Kantō and suppressing rebellions")]

See also: [Siege of Odawara (1590)](/wiki/Siege_of_Odawara_(1590) "Siege of Odawara (1590)") and [Edo](/wiki/Edo "Edo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Odawara-jo.jpg/250px-Odawara-jo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Odawara-jo.jpg)

Odawara castle in 2024

In 1590 May, Ieyasu participated in the [campaign against the Hōjō clan](/wiki/Siege_of_Odawara_(1590) "Siege of Odawara (1590)"). Odawara, which was the last Hōjō clan stronghold, saw almost no significant military action, with the exception of Ii Naomasa's night raid attack. This happened after a group of miners from [Kai Province](/wiki/Kai_Province "Kai Province") dug under the castle walls, allowing troops under Naomasa to enter and engage the enemy.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurnbull1998241-191) After the surrender of the Hōjō clan, Ieyasu sent Naomasa and Sakakibara Yasumasa with 1,500 soldiers to witness the seppuku of the defeated enemy generals, [Hōjō Ujimasa](/wiki/H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_Ujimasa "Hōjō Ujimasa") and [Hōjō Ujiteru](/wiki/H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_Ujiteru "Hōjō Ujiteru").[[178]](#cite_note-192) As result of his distinguished service during the campaign, Naomasa was awarded with an increase in his domain to 120,000 koku.[[179]](#cite_note-高崎市史_Volume_1;_Naomasa_Odawara-193) Daidōji Masashige, a senior Hōjō clan retainer, was also forced to commit seppuku by Hideyoshi, however, his children were spared from execution at the behest of Ieyasu. The eldest son, Daidōji Naoshige, became Ieyasu's vassal after the death of Ujinao.[[180]](#cite_note-194)
