On October 28 of the same year, a [massive revolt](/w/index.php?title=Waga-Hienuki_Rebellion&action=edit&redlink=1 "Waga-Hienuki Rebellion (page does not exist)") [[jp](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%92%8C%E8%B3%80%E3%83%BB%E7%A8%97%E8%B2%AB%E4%B8%80%E6%8F%86 "jp:和賀・稗貫一揆")] against the Toyotomi government in [Mutsu Province](/wiki/Mutsu_Province "Mutsu Province") was incited by Hienuki Hirotada and Waga Yoshitada. In response, Hideyoshi sent a punitive expedition with an army 30,000 strong led by Ieyasu Tokugawa, [Toyotomi Hidetsugu](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidetsugu "Toyotomi Hidetsugu"), [Date Masamune](/wiki/Date_Masamune "Date Masamune"), [Ishida Mitsunari](/wiki/Ishida_Mitsunari "Ishida Mitsunari"), [Ōtani Yoshitsugu](/wiki/%C5%8Ctani_Yoshitsugu "Ōtani Yoshitsugu"), [Gamō Ujisato](/wiki/Gam%C5%8D_Ujisato "Gamō Ujisato"), [Uesugi Kagekatsu](/wiki/Uesugi_Kagekatsu "Uesugi Kagekatsu"), [Satake Yoshishige](/wiki/Satake_Yoshishige "Satake Yoshishige"), and [Maeda Toshiie](/wiki/Maeda_Toshiie "Maeda Toshiie") in order to pacify the rebellion.[[181]](#cite_note-Aomori_Wagahienuki-195)

In 1591, Ieyasu gave up control of his five provinces ([Mikawa](/wiki/Mikawa_Province "Mikawa Province"), [Tōtōmi](/wiki/T%C5%8Dt%C5%8Dmi_Province "Tōtōmi Province"), [Suruga](/wiki/Suruga_Province "Suruga Province"), [Shinano](/wiki/Shinano_Province "Shinano Province"), and [Kai](/wiki/Kai_Province "Kai Province")) and moved all his soldiers and vassals to his eight new provinces in the Kantō region. The proclamation of this decision happened on the same day as Hideyoshi entered Odawara castle after the Hōjō clan formally surrendered.[[182]](#cite_note-Ando_Yuichiro;_Tokugawa_Kanto-196) The moment Ieyasu was appointed to rule Kantō, he immediately assigned his premier vassals, including Ii Naomasa, Honda Tadakatsu, Sakakibara Yasumasa, and Sakai Ietsugu, the son of Sakai Tadatsugu, to each control a large area of the former Hōjō clan territories in Kantō. Historians saw this step as aimed at bringing order to the newly subdued population of the area, while also guarding the eastern domains from any influence or threat from the [Satomi clan](/wiki/Satomi_clan "Satomi clan"), which had not yet submitted to Toyotomi rule at that time.[[183]](#cite_note-197)[[184]](#cite_note-198) Meanwhile, Ieyasu himself established his personal seat of power in Edo town, which at that time was an underdeveloped town in Kantō.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENakamura2010210-199)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Kanto_Region_in_Japan.svg/250px-Kanto_Region_in_Japan.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kanto_Region_in_Japan.svg)

Kanto Region in Japan

Historian Adam Sadler saw this step as the riskiest Ieyasu ever made—to leave his home province and rely on the uncertain loyalty of the former Hōjō clan samurai in Kantō. In the end, it worked out brilliantly for Ieyasu. He reformed the Kantō region, controlled and pacified the Hōjō samurai, and improved the underlying economic infrastructure. Also, because Kantō was somewhat isolated from the rest of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), Ieyasu was able to ally with daimyo of north-eastern Japan including [Date Masamune](/wiki/Date_Masamune "Date Masamune"), [Mogami Yoshiaki](/wiki/Mogami_Yoshiaki "Mogami Yoshiaki"), [Satake Yoshishige](/wiki/Satake_Yoshishige "Satake Yoshishige") and [Nanbu Nobunao](/wiki/Nanbu_Nobunao "Nanbu Nobunao"); he was also able to maintain a unique level of autonomy from [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi")'s rule. Within a few years, Ieyasu had become the second most powerful daimyo in Japan. An anecdotal proverb says, "Ieyasu won the Empire by retreating."[[186]](#cite_note-200)

Modern Japanese historians reject that this was a deliberate move by Ieyasu since it was an order from Hideyoshi.[[182]](#cite_note-Ando_Yuichiro;_Tokugawa_Kanto-196) Nevertheless, [Watanabe Daimon](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B8%A1%E9%82%8A%E5%A4%A7%E9%96%80 "jp:渡邊大門") stated that, while the general opinion was that Ieyasu was reluctant about his transfer to Kantō, this perception was unfounded. Instead, Daimon suspected that Ieyasu actually responded to this transfer positively as he saw potential in making undeveloped Edo into his seat of power.[[187]](#cite_note-201)[[o]](#cite_note-203)

Similarly, Andō Yūichirō viewed this transfer as an advantage for the Tokugawa regime in the long run, as this move was not only doubled the territory that he controlled, but it further added numerous new vassals in Kantō to the already impressive political and military power of the Tokugawa regime, which had already absorbed the armies of the Imagawa and Takeda clans before. Yūichirō added that aside from the samurai from Imagawa, Takeda, and Hōjō, the Mikawa samurai clans who were traditional followers of the Tokugawa clan also lost their sense of independence after being transferred into a new unfamiliar territory, which increased their sense of dependence on Ieyasu, in effect further minimizing the possibility of them going renegade and betraying Ieyasu, as Ishikawa Kazumasa had done several years earlier.[[182]](#cite_note-Ando_Yuichiro;_Tokugawa_Kanto-196)

After the Waga-Hienuki rebellion, the [Kunohe rebellion](/wiki/Kunohe_rebellion "Kunohe rebellion") led by Kunohe Masazane broke out on March 13, 1591. This caused the punitive expedition army to split their forces as Ieyasu, Naomasa, Ujisato, and some commanders changed their focus to suppress Masazane's rebellion first.[[189]](#cite_note-Hideyoshi_rebellion;_Seiji_Kobayashi-204)[[190]](#cite_note-205) Ieyasu, who at that time was also busy suppressing rebellions with his main commanders, including Sakakibara Yasumasa, Ii Naomasa, and Honda Tadakatsu, arrived at Iwatesawa, Tamazukuri district, Mutsu (modern day [Iwadeyama](/wiki/Iwadeyama,_Miyagi "Iwadeyama, Miyagi"), [Miyagi Prefecture](/wiki/Miyagi_Prefecture "Miyagi Prefecture")) on August 18, where he camped until October and led the troops.[[191]](#cite_note-所沢市史,_Volume_10;_Kunohe_rebellion_&_Kanto-206)

During the operation against the Kunohe clan rebels, Naomasa Ii became the vanguard with [Nanbu Nobunao](/wiki/Nanbu_Nobunao "Nanbu Nobunao"). As they advanced towards Kunohe castle, they faced a small force of Kunohe rebels which they easily defeated.[[189]](#cite_note-Hideyoshi_rebellion;_Seiji_Kobayashi-204) Naomasa suggested to besiege the castle until they surrendered, which was met with agreement.[[192]](#cite_note-207)

On 4 September, the rebels executed the prisoners inside the castle and incited mass suicide after setting fire to the castle. The castle burned for three days and nights killing all within.[[193]](#cite_note-Turnbull;Kunohe-208)[[189]](#cite_note-Hideyoshi_rebellion;_Seiji_Kobayashi-204)[[181]](#cite_note-Aomori_Wagahienuki-195) The rebellions were suppressed by June 20, with Waga Yoshitada being slain in battle,[[194]](#cite_note-209) while Hienuki Hirotada was sentenced to "*[Kaieki law](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%94%B9%E6%98%93 "jp:改易")*" which stated that his and his clan's status and rights as samurai were stripped.[[195]](#cite_note-210)

As the operation ended, Ieyasu returned to Edo on October 29 and began managing his new territory in the Kantō region.[[191]](#cite_note-所沢市史,_Volume_10;_Kunohe_rebellion_&_Kanto-206) Ieyasu managed to establish his home base in Kantō, and built sustainable economic infrastructure in the region.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENishimoto2010141-211) Ieyasu also employed [Gotō Shōzaburō](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%BE%8C%E8%97%A4%E5%BA%84%E4%B8%89%E9%83%8E "jp:後藤庄三郎"), head of the gold mining and metal industries of the Sengoku period, to mint gold coins and establish a bank-like institution for the Tokugawa clan's government.[[197]](#cite_note-Hamada_Kōichirō_Ieyasu_vassals-212)

In 1592, Toyotomi Hideyoshi [invaded Korea](/wiki/Japanese_invasions_of_Korea_(1592%E2%80%9398) "Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–98)") as a prelude to his plan to attack [China](/wiki/China "China"). The Tokugawa clan samurai didn't take part in this campaign, as Hideyoshi had ordered the eastern provinces' daimyo including Ieyasu, Uesugi Kagekatsu, and Date Masamune to maintain logistical support for the war effort in [Nagoya Castle](/wiki/Nagoya_Castle "Nagoya Castle").[[p]](#cite_note-214)

In 1593, Toyotomi Hideyoshi fathered a son and heir, [Toyotomi Hideyori](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyori "Toyotomi Hideyori"). Later, though still in early 1593, Ieyasu himself was summoned to Hideyoshi's court in [Nagoya](/wiki/Nagoya_Castle_(Hizen_Province) "Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)") (in [Kyushu](/wiki/Kyushu "Kyushu"), not the similarly spelled city in [Owari Province](/wiki/Owari_Province "Owari Province")) as a military advisor and was given command of troops meant as reserves for the Korean campaign. Ieyasu stayed in Nagoya intermittedly for the next five years.[[47]](#cite_note-Sansom_1961-54)

In July 1595, the "[Toyotomi Hidetsugu](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidetsugu "Toyotomi Hidetsugu") Incident" occurred. In response to this major incident shaking the Toyotomi government, Hideyoshi ordered various daimyo to come to Kyoto in an attempt to calm the situation. Ieyasu also came to Kyoto on Hideyoshi's orders. From this point on, Ieyasu spent longer and longer periods in Fushimi Castle than in his underdeveloped residence, Edo Castle. Due to this chain of events, Ieyasu's position in the Toyotomi government had risen, but by being at the center of the government, Ieyasu was able to learn directly about the central government's political system.[[199]](#cite_note-owada2007-215)

## Ruler of Japan (1598–1603)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Ruler of Japan (1598–1603)")]

Main article: [Council of Five Elders](/wiki/Council_of_Five_Elders "Council of Five Elders")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Joint_letter_of_Five_Elders_%28of_Toyotomi%29.jpg/330px-Joint_letter_of_Five_Elders_%28of_Toyotomi%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joint_letter_of_Five_Elders_(of_Toyotomi).jpg)

The signatures of the Five Elders. From the top left, Uesugi Kagekatsu and Mori Terumoto, from the bottom left, Ukita Hideie, Maeda Toshiie, and Tokugawa Ieyasu. The bottom row is upside down.

In 1598, with his health clearly failing, Hideyoshi called a meeting that would determine the [Council of Five Elders](/wiki/Council_of_Five_Elders "Council of Five Elders"), who would be responsible for ruling on behalf of his son after his death. The five that were chosen as [tairō](/wiki/Tair%C5%8D "Tairō") (regents) for Hideyori were [Maeda Toshiie](/wiki/Maeda_Toshiie "Maeda Toshiie"), [Mōri Terumoto](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Terumoto "Mōri Terumoto"), [Ukita Hideie](/wiki/Ukita_Hideie "Ukita Hideie"), [Uesugi Kagekatsu](/wiki/Uesugi_Kagekatsu "Uesugi Kagekatsu"), and Ieyasu, who was the most powerful of the five. This change in the pre-Sekigahara power structure became pivotal as Ieyasu turned his attention towards [Kansai](/wiki/Kansai_region "Kansai region"); at the same time, other ambitious (albeit ultimately unrealized) plans, such as the Tokugawa initiative establishing official relations with [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") (modern-day [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico")), continued to unfold and advance.[[200]](#cite_note-216)[[201]](#cite_note-217)

### Death of Hideyoshi and Toshiie

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Death of Hideyoshi and Toshiie")]

Toyotomi Hideyoshi, after three more months of deteriorating health, died on September 18, 1598. He was nominally succeeded by his young son [Hideyori](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyori "Toyotomi Hideyori") but as he was just five years old, the real power was in the hands of the regents.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] There were several incidents involving Ieyasu after the death of Hideyoshi:

* The government of Japan under Toyotomi's rule had an incident when seven military generals ([Fukushima Masanori](/wiki/Fukushima_Masanori "Fukushima Masanori"), [Katō Kiyomasa](/wiki/Kat%C5%8D_Kiyomasa "Katō Kiyomasa"), [Ikeda Terumasa](/wiki/Ikeda_Terumasa "Ikeda Terumasa"), [Hosokawa Tadaoki](/wiki/Hosokawa_Tadaoki "Hosokawa Tadaoki"), [Asano Yoshinaga](/wiki/Asano_Yoshinaga_(Lord_of_Hiroshima) "Asano Yoshinaga (Lord of Hiroshima)"), [Katō Yoshiaki](/wiki/Kat%C5%8D_Yoshiaki "Katō Yoshiaki"), and [Kuroda Nagamasa](/wiki/Kuroda_Nagamasa "Kuroda Nagamasa")) came into conflict with Ishida Mitsunari. It was said that the reason for this was that they were dissastisfied with Mitsunari because he wrote poor assessments and underreported their achievements during the [Imjin War](/wiki/Japanese_invasions_of_Korea_(1592%E2%80%931598) "Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)") against Korea and the Chinese empire.[[202]](#cite_note-7_generals_conspiracy;_Mizuno-218) At first, these generals gathered at Kiyomasa's mansion in [Osaka Castle](/wiki/Osaka_Castle "Osaka Castle"), and from there they marched to Mitsunari's mansion. However, Mitsunari learned of this through a report from a servant of [Toyotomi Hideyori](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyori "Toyotomi Hideyori") named Jiemon Kuwajima and he fled to [Satake Yoshinobu](/wiki/Satake_Yoshinobu "Satake Yoshinobu")'s mansion together with [Shima Sakon](/wiki/Shima_Sakon "Shima Sakon") and others to hide.[[202]](#cite_note-7_generals_conspiracy;_Mizuno-218) When the seven generals found that Mitsunari was not in the mansion, they searched the mansions of various feudal lords in Osaka Castle, and Katō's army approached the Satake residence. Mitsunari and his party then escaped from the Satake residence and barricaded themselves at [Fushimi Castle](/wiki/Fushimi_Castle "Fushimi Castle").[[203]](#cite_note-kasaya2000-219)

The next day, the seven generals surrounded Fushimi Castle with their soldiers as they knew Mitsunari was hiding there. Ieyasu, who was in charge of political affairs in Fushimi Castle at that moment, attempted to arbitrate the situation. The seven generals requested Ieyasu hand over Mitsunari, which Ieyasu refused. Ieyasu then negotiated a promise to let Mitsunari retire and to review the assessment of the Battle of Ulsan Castle in Korea. Ieyasu had his second son, [Yūki Hideyasu](/wiki/Y%C5%ABki_Hideyasu "Yūki Hideyasu"), escort Mitsunari to Sawayama Castle.[[204]](#cite_note-220)[[q]](#cite_note-222)[[r]](#cite_note-225)

* Tokugawa ordered his general, Sakakibara Yasumasa, to lead an army from Kantō to camp in Seta, Ōmi Province as a means of showing off and intimidating the bureaucratic faction which was led by Ishida Mitsunari, because Ōmi was the traditional hometown of the Mitsunari clan.[[208]](#cite_note-setaarmySakakibara-226)
* In 1599, a riot occurred within the [Ukita clan](/wiki/Ukita_clan "Ukita clan") when several of Ukita clan vassals, including Togawa Tatsuyasu,[[209]](#cite_note-227) Sadatsuna Oka and others, rebelled against Hideie.[[210]](#cite_note-hideieriot-228) At first, Ieyasu sent his general [Sakakibara Yasumasa](/wiki/Sakakibara_Yasumasa "Sakakibara Yasumasa") to mediate the disputes between Ukita Hideie and his various rebellious vassals. However, the situation was not resolved for a long time so Ieyasu ordered Yasumasa to return to his post and decided to resolve the case himself. Ieyasu managed to solve the case and averted a civil war between two factions. However, in the aftermath of this incident many of Hideie retainers such as [Sakazaki Naomori](/wiki/Sakazaki_Naomori "Sakazaki Naomori") changed their allegiance to Tokugawa Ieyasu and left Hideie. These defections caused massive setbacks for the Ukita clan politically and militarily, while strengthening Ieyasu.[[211]](#cite_note-豊臣期の宇喜多氏と宇喜多秀家;_Sakakibara_Yasumasa-229)
* In April 1599, Ieyasu cited Hideyoshi's will as pretext for him to review the decision regarding the [Mōri clan](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_clan "Mōri clan") territories that Mitsunari had pushed through, and pressed [Mōri Terumoto](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Terumoto "Mōri Terumoto") to allocate part of [Nagato Province](/wiki/Nagato_Province "Nagato Province") and [Suō Province](/wiki/Su%C5%8D_Province "Suō Province") to [Mōri Hidemoto](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Hidemoto "Mōri Hidemoto").[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitsunari2016250–251-230) In June, Ieyasu's manifesto to curtail Terumoto's domains was implemented, as Hidemoto had been given the former [Mōri Motokiyo](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Motokiyo "Mōri Motokiyo") territory of Nagato, Yoshiki District in Suō, Aki, and Bingo, leaving [Kikkawa Hiroie](/wiki/Kikkawa_Hiroie "Kikkawa Hiroie")'s territory intact, and returned [Kobayakawa Takakage](/wiki/Kobayakawa_Takakage "Kobayakawa Takakage")'s estate to Terumoto.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitsunari2016251-231)
* Ieyasu had his general and diplomat, Ii Naomasa, establish contact with the scions of [Kuroda clan](/wiki/Kuroda_clan "Kuroda clan"), [Kuroda Yoshitaka](/wiki/Kuroda_Yoshitaka "Kuroda Yoshitaka") and Kuroda Nagamasa, and gained their political support.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoda2007-232)
* On September 12, 1599, when Ieyasu returned to Fushimi castle from Osaka castle, there was an alleged assassination attempt by three Toyotomi Hideyoshi vassals named Katsuhisa Hijikata, [Asano Nagamasa](/wiki/Asano_Nagamasa "Asano Nagamasa"), and [Ōno Harunaga](/wiki/%C5%8Cno_Harunaga "Ōno Harunaga"). Their attempt to assassinate Ieyasu failed due to Ieyasu's tight security and bodyguards. When they were apprehended, further investigation also linked the assassination attempt with [Maeda Toshimasa](/wiki/Maeda_Toshimasa "Maeda Toshimasa"), son of Maeda Toshiie. Ieyasu consulted with [Honda Masanobu](/wiki/Honda_Masanobu "Honda Masanobu") about the proper punishment for each conspirator, Masanobu instead advised Ieyasu to show leniency towards the three perpetrators. In the end, Ieyasu accepted Masanobu's counsel and decided that he would not execute them, in exchange, he placed Hijikata and Katsuhisa on house arrest on northern side of the Kantō region. Meanwhile, Nagamasa was given a far more lenient punishment than his compatriots by only being ordered to move his residence, Musashi Fuchū, as Ieyasu saw Nagamasa had an important political position in the government. As for Toshimasa, Ieyasu prepared a harsh punishment and he prepared to dispatch his army to Kaga in order to subjugate Toshinaga, who was one of the Five Elders. In response, Toshinaga sent his subordinate, Nagatomo Yokoyama, to Ieyasu and immediately apologized to Ieyasu. Furthermore, he sent his mother, Hoshunin, as a hostage to Edo, and arranged for his adopted heir, Toshitsune, to marry Hidetada's daughter, Tamahime. Due to those four men later supporting Ieyasu in the Sekigahara war against Mitsunari, modern historian Daimon Watanabe saw Tokugawa's lenient attitude towards his would-be assassins as a political move to gather more allies to would support him in the future war.[[215]](#cite_note-233)
* Tokugawa married his sixth son, [Matsudaira Tadateru](/wiki/Matsudaira_Tadateru "Matsudaira Tadateru"), to [Irohahime](/wiki/Irohahime "Irohahime"), the first daughter of Date Masamune.[[216]](#cite_note-234)

Ieyasu also gained support from Mogami Yoshiaki, brother-in-law of Masamune and a powerful eastern daimyo, who had held a grudge against the Toyotomi clan since Hideyoshi executed his daughter in the Hidetsugu Incident of 1595.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKanie1990443-235) Hidetsugu had been accused of treason and forced to commit seppuku at [Mount Kōya](/wiki/Mount_K%C5%8Dya "Mount Kōya"). In the aftermath of this incident, his entire family was also executed at Sanjogawara. Yoshiaki's daughter, Komahime, who was only 15 years old and had recently married Hidetsugu, was executed as a result. Yoshiaki begged for her life to be spared as she hadn't even met Hidetsugu yet, but his request was refused. Komahime was beheaded along with the others, and her body dumped in the Sanjogawara River. As result of the execution, Yoshiaki's wife, Osaki-dono, was struck with deep grief by the sudden death of her daughter and died on August 16. After this, Yoshiaki grew closer to Ieyasu and became one of his strongest supporters.[[218]](#cite_note-Kenji_Matsuo;_Mogami_Yoshiaki-236)

* Another incident occurred in 1595 with the [Shimazu clan](/wiki/Shimazu_clan "Shimazu clan"), when [Shimazu Tadatsune](/wiki/Shimazu_Tadatsune "Shimazu Tadatsune"), the third son of [Shimazu Yoshihiro](/wiki/Shimazu_Yoshihiro "Shimazu Yoshihiro") and heir to the main Shimazu family, assassinated a clan's chief vassal named Ijuin Tadamune. The background to this incident seems to be that Tadamune, who was on close terms with Ishida Mitsunari, had infringed on the Shimazu clan's domain. However, the incident did not end there, and Tadamune's eldest son, Ijuin Tadamasa, started a rebellion. As the civil war reached deadlock, Ieyasu mediated the two sides, which resulted in Tadamasa surrendering in March 1600.[[219]](#cite_note-Shimazu_Sekigahara-237)

### Conflict with Mitsunari

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Conflict with Mitsunari")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Kabuto_Tokugawa_Ieyasu.jpg/250px-Kabuto_Tokugawa_Ieyasu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kabuto_Tokugawa_Ieyasu.jpg)

The [kabuto](/wiki/Kabuto "Kabuto") (helmet) of Tokugawa Ieyasu

Main articles: [Siege of Shiroishi](/wiki/Siege_of_Shiroishi "Siege of Shiroishi"), [Siege of Hasedō](/wiki/Siege_of_Hased%C5%8D "Siege of Hasedō"), [Battle of Gifu Castle](/wiki/Battle_of_Gifu_Castle "Battle of Gifu Castle"), [Siege of Fushimi](/wiki/Siege_of_Fushimi "Siege of Fushimi"), and [Siege of Ueda](/wiki/Siege_of_Ueda "Siege of Ueda")

Meanwhile, opposition to Ieyasu centered around [Ishida Mitsunari](/wiki/Ishida_Mitsunari "Ishida Mitsunari"), one of Hideyoshi's *[Go-Bugyō](/wiki/Go-Bugy%C5%8D "Go-Bugyō")*, or top administrators of Hideyoshi's government, and a powerful daimyo who was not one of the regents. Mitsunari plotted Ieyasu's death and news of this plot reached some of Ieyasu's generals. They attempted to kill Mitsunari but he fled and gained protection from Ieyasu. It is not clear why Ieyasu protected a powerful enemy from his own men, but Ieyasu was a master strategist and may have concluded that he would be better off with Mitsunari leading the enemy army rather than one of the more legitimate regents.[[220]](#cite_note-238)

Nearly all of Japan's daimyo and samurai were now split into two factions; the Western Army (Mitsunari's group) and the Eastern Army (Ieyasu's group). Ieyasu had the support of the anti-Mitsunari group, and formed them into his potential allies. Ieyasu's allies were [Katō Kiyomasa](/wiki/Kat%C5%8D_Kiyomasa "Katō Kiyomasa"), [Fukushima Masanori](/wiki/Fukushima_Masanori "Fukushima Masanori"), [Mogami Yoshiaki](/wiki/Mogami_Yoshiaki "Mogami Yoshiaki"), [Hachisuka Iemasa](/wiki/Hachisuka_Iemasa "Hachisuka Iemasa"), the [Kuroda clan](/wiki/Kuroda_clan "Kuroda clan"), the [Hosokawa clan](/wiki/Hosokawa_clan "Hosokawa clan") and many daimyo from eastern Japan. Mitsunari had allied himself with three other regents: [Ukita Hideie](/wiki/Ukita_Hideie "Ukita Hideie"), [Mōri Terumoto](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Terumoto "Mōri Terumoto"), and [Uesugi Kagekatsu](/wiki/Uesugi_Kagekatsu "Uesugi Kagekatsu"), as well as with [Ōtani Yoshitsugu](/wiki/%C5%8Ctani_Yoshitsugu "Ōtani Yoshitsugu"), the [Chosokabe clan](/wiki/Chosokabe_clan "Chosokabe clan"), the [Shimazu clan](/wiki/Shimazu_clan "Shimazu clan") and many daimyo from the western end of [Honshū](/wiki/Honshu "Honshu").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

War became imminent when Uesugi Kagekatsu, one of Hideyoshi's appointed regents, defied Ieyasu by building up his military at [Aizu](/wiki/Aizu "Aizu"). When Ieyasu officially condemned him and demanded that he come to Kyoto to explain himself, Kagekatsu's chief advisor, [Naoe Kanetsugu](/wiki/Naoe_Kanetsugu "Naoe Kanetsugu"), responded with a counter-condemnation that mocked Ieyasu's abuses and violations of Hideyoshi's rules. This infuriated Ieyasu.

In July 1600, Ieyasu was back in [Edo](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle") and his allies moved their armies to defeat the [Uesugi clan](/wiki/Uesugi_clan "Uesugi clan"), which were accused of planning to revolt against the [Toyotomi](/wiki/Toyotomi_clan "Toyotomi clan") administration. On September 8, Ieyasu received information that Mitsunari [had captured Fushimi castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Fushimi "Siege of Fushimi") and his allies had moved their army against Ieyasu. Ieyasu held a meeting with the Eastern Army daimyo, and they agreed to follow Ieyasu. Later, on September 15, Mitsunari's Western army arrived at [Ogaki Castle](/wiki/Ogaki_Castle "Ogaki Castle"). On September 29, Ieyasu's Eastern Army [took Gifu Castle](/wiki/Battle_of_Gifu_Castle "Battle of Gifu Castle"). On October 7, Ieyasu and his allies marched along the [Tōkaidō](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_(road) "Tōkaidō (road)"), while his son [Hidetada](/wiki/Tokugawa_Hidetada "Tokugawa Hidetada") went along through [Nakasendō](/wiki/Nakasend%C5%8D "Nakasendō") with 38,000 soldiers (a battle against [Sanada Masayuki](/wiki/Sanada_Masayuki "Sanada Masayuki") in [Shinano Province](/wiki/Shinano_Province "Shinano Province") delayed Hidetada's forces, and they did not arrive in time for the main [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara")).[[221]](#cite_note-sakakibaraUeda-239)

### Battle of Sekigahara

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Battle of Sekigahara")]

Main article: [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Sekigahara_Tokugawa%27s_Final_Encampment.jpg/250px-Sekigahara_Tokugawa%27s_Final_Encampment.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sekigahara_Tokugawa%27s_Final_Encampment.jpg)

Tokugawa Ieyasu last position during the battle

The Battle of Sekigahara was the biggest [battle](/wiki/List_of_Japanese_battles "List of Japanese battles") as well as one of the most important in Japanese feudal history. It began on October 21, 1600. The Eastern Army led by Tokugawa Ieyasu initially numbered 75,000 men, with the Western Army at a strength of 120,000 men under [Ishida Mitsunari](/wiki/Ishida_Mitsunari "Ishida Mitsunari"). Ieyasu had also secretly acquired a supply of [arquebuses](/wiki/Arquebus "Arquebus").[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Knowing that the [Tokugawa](/wiki/Tokugawa_clan "Tokugawa clan") forces were heading towards [Osaka](/wiki/Osaka "Osaka"), Mitsunari decided to abandon his positions and marched to Sekigahara. Even though the Western Army had tremendous tactical advantages, Ieyasu had already been in contact with many of the daimyo in the Western Army for months, promising them land and leniency after the battle should they switch sides. Ieyasu had also secretly communicated with [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi")'s nephew, [Kobayakawa Hideaki](/wiki/Kobayakawa_Hideaki "Kobayakawa Hideaki"). With a total of 170,000 soldiers facing each other, the Battle of Sekigahara ensued and ended with an overwhelming Tokugawa victory.[[222]](#cite_note-240) At the conclusion of the battle, Ieyasu marched to Osaka castle, where [Mōri Terumoto](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Terumoto "Mōri Terumoto"), the grand commander of Western army, surrendered to him.[[223]](#cite_note-241)

The Western bloc quickly collapsed, and over the next few days Ishida Mitsunari and other western leaders such as [Konishi Yukinaga](/wiki/Konishi_Yukinaga "Konishi Yukinaga") and [Ankokuji Ekei](/wiki/Ankokuji_Ekei "Ankokuji Ekei") were captured and executed.[[224]](#cite_note-242) However, Ieyasu was angry at his son Hidetada, whose army was late to arrive, leading to an unexpectedly long siege against Ueda castle. Sakakibara Yasumasa offered an explanation and testified in defense of Hidetada.[[221]](#cite_note-sakakibaraUeda-239) Meanwhile, Ieyasu pardoned his enemies who defended Ueda castle, including [Sanada Masayuki](/wiki/Sanada_Masayuki "Sanada Masayuki") and [Sanada Yukimura](/wiki/Sanada_Yukimura "Sanada Yukimura"), at the behest of Ii Naomasa and [Sanada Nobuyuki](/wiki/Sanada_Nobuyuki "Sanada Nobuyuki").[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE野田2007典拠史料は「真田家武功口上之覚」（『真田家文書』中巻、1982年-243)

### Aftermath of Sekigahara battle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Aftermath of Sekigahara battle")]

Ieyasu redistributed the domain fiefs of all the daimyo lords who supported him during the war, such as increasing Ii Naomasa domain to 180,000 koku.[[226]](#cite_note-徳川四天王_家康に天下を取らせた男たち;_Naomasa_Ii-244) [Ikeda Terumasa](/wiki/Ikeda_Terumasa "Ikeda Terumasa")'s to 520,000 Koku.[[227]](#cite_note-Japanese_Tea_Ceremony_Cha-No-Yu-245) [Tōdō Takatora](/wiki/T%C5%8Dd%C5%8D_Takatora "Tōdō Takatora") got a new domain which was assessed at a total of 200,000 koku,[[83]](#cite_note-kotobank-91) [Yuki Hideyasu](/wiki/Y%C5%ABki_Hideyasu "Yūki Hideyasu") went from 101,000 to 569,000 koku, Matsudaira Tadayoshi went from 100,000 to 520,000 koku, [Gamō Hideyuki](/wiki/Gam%C5%8D_Hideyuki "Gamō Hideyuki") increased from 180,000 to 600,000 koku, Maeda Toshinaga went from 835,000 to 1,100,000 koku, [Katō Kiyomasa](/wiki/Kat%C5%8D_Kiyomasa "Katō Kiyomasa")'s domain grew from 195,000 to 515,000 koku, and Kuroda Nagamasa's grew from 180,000 to 523,000 koku. Meanwhile, Fukushima Masanori had his 200,000 koku domain increased to 498,000 in [Aki, Hiroshima](/wiki/Aki_District,_Hiroshima "Aki District, Hiroshima"). Ieyasu also promoted many of his own hereditary vassals to domains of at least 10,000 koku for their stipends.[[228]](#cite_note-IeyasuSekigahara;_Watanabe_Daimon1-246) After the battle, Ieyasu left some Western Army daimyo unharmed, such as the [Shimazu clan](/wiki/Shimazu_clan "Shimazu clan"), but others were completely destroyed. [Toyotomi Hideyori](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyori "Toyotomi Hideyori") (the son of Hideyoshi) lost most of his territory which was under management of western daimyo, and was degraded to an ordinary daimyo, rather than a [Sesshō or Kampaku](/wiki/Sessh%C5%8D_and_Kampaku "Sesshō and Kampaku") (regent) of the Japanese empire. In later years, the vassals who had pledged allegiance to Ieyasu before Sekigahara became known as the *[fudai daimyō](/wiki/Fudai_daimy%C5%8D "Fudai daimyō")*, while those who pledged allegiance to him after the battle (after his power was unquestioned) were known as *[tozama daimyō](/wiki/Tozama_daimy%C5%8D "Tozama daimyō")*. *Tozama daimyō* were considered inferior to *fudai daimyō*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This redistribution of domains was done verbally, instead of by formal [letter of intent](/wiki/Letter_of_intent "Letter of intent"). Historian Watanabe Daimon suspected this was because Ieyasu was still wary of the existence of Toyotomi clan which had been inherited by Toyotomi Hideyori.[[228]](#cite_note-IeyasuSekigahara;_Watanabe_Daimon1-246)

On September 20, Ieyasu entered Otsu castle, where he welcomed and met with Kyōnyo, the head of [Hongan-ji](/wiki/Hongan-ji "Hongan-ji") temple at that time, with [Kanamori Nagachika](/wiki/Kanamori_Nagachika "Kanamori Nagachika") as an intermediary.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUeba2005178-180-247)[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEŌkuwa201369-71,154-248)[[231]](#cite_note-249)[[232]](#cite_note-250) He met Ieyasu again the following year (1601), and Ieyasu visited Kyōnyo on July 5 and August 16. However, for the rest of 1601, the relationship between them grew worse as Kyōnyo was accused of pro-Mitsunari sympathy.[[233]](#cite_note-251)[[234]](#cite_note-252) It was only in February 1602 that the communication between Ieyasu with Kyōnyo opened again. This was after the intercession from aide [Honda Masanobu](/wiki/Honda_Masanobu "Honda Masanobu"), where the three of them discussed the condition of the Hongan-ji temple development after the split of the sect into two factions. Ieyasu worried the strife within the temple could affect the stability of Japan after the [Sekigahara war](/wiki/Sekigahara_Campaign "Sekigahara Campaign").[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEŌkuwa201371–72-253)

In 1602, Ieyasu changed his surname from **"Minamoto"** to **"Fujiwara"**. The reason for this changing was because [Emperor Go-Yōzei](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Y%C5%8Dzei "Emperor Go-Yōzei") wanted to appoint Ieyasu to be a court noble. However, there was no precedent in the Tokugawa bloodline as the [Minamoto clan](/wiki/Minamoto_clan "Minamoto clan") which Ieyasu ancestry claimed, was a samurai clan rather than a noble family. To resolve this problem, a fabrication was made that said the Tokugawa clan also descended from [Fujiwara clan](/wiki/Fujiwara_clan "Fujiwara clan"), which was a noble family. By changing his name to Fujiwara, Ieyasu was able to be appointed to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank.[[s]](#cite_note-255)

## Establishment of the Tokugawa shogunate (1603–1616)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Establishment of the Tokugawa shogunate (1603–1616)")]

Main article: [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Shogun-Tokugawa-Ieyasu.png/250px-Shogun-Tokugawa-Ieyasu.png)](/wiki/File:Shogun-Tokugawa-Ieyasu.png)

[Ukiyo-e](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e") of Tokugawa Ieyasu

On March 24, 1603, Tokugawa Ieyasu received the title of *shōgun* from [Emperor Go-Yōzei](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Y%C5%8Dzei "Emperor Go-Yōzei").[[237]](#cite_note-256) In the same day, he also appointed as [Minister of the Right](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Right "Minister of the Right"), *Overall Head of the Minamoto-Genji clan* ([Genji Chōja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%BA%90%E6%B0%8F%E9%95%B7%E8%80%85 "ja:源氏長者")), and also *Chief [Attendant](/wiki/Zuijin "Zuijin") of the Bullock-cart Proclamation*.[[238]](#cite_note-257)

Ieyasu was 60 years old and had outlasted all the other great men of his times: Oda Nobunaga, Takeda Shingen, Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and [Uesugi Kenshin](/wiki/Uesugi_Kenshin "Uesugi Kenshin"). As *shōgun*, he used his remaining years to create and solidify the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate"), which ushered in the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period"), and was the third shogunal government (after the [Kamakura](/wiki/Kamakura_shogunate "Kamakura shogunate") and the [Ashikaga](/wiki/Ashikaga_shogunate "Ashikaga shogunate")). He claimed descent from the Minamoto clan who had founded the Kamakura shogunate, by way of the [Nitta clan](/wiki/Nitta_clan "Nitta clan"). His descendants would marry into the [Taira clan](/wiki/Taira_clan "Taira clan") and the Fujiwara clan.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-9)

Following a well established Japanese pattern, Ieyasu abdicated his official position as *shōgun* in 1605. His successor was his son and heir, [Tokugawa Hidetada](/wiki/Tokugawa_Hidetada "Tokugawa Hidetada"). There may have been several factors that contributed to his decision, including his desire to avoid being tied up in ceremonial duties, to make it harder for his enemies to attack his real power center, and to secure a smoother succession for his son.[[239]](#cite_note-258)

In 1604, Tōdō Takatora and Date Masamune advised the shogunate government to introduce a rule across Japan that each feudal lord was obliged to maintain a residence in Edo, the capital of the shogunate, which Ieyasu immediately accepted and implemented officially.[[240]](#cite_note-259)

### *Ōgosho* (1605–1616)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Ōgosho (1605–1616)")]

Main article: [Edo Castle](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Edo_P_detail.jpg/250px-Edo_P_detail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edo_P_detail.jpg)

[Edo Castle](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle") from a 17th-century painting

From 1605 onwards, Ieyasu, who had retired from the official position of shogun and became a retired *shōgun* (大御所, *ōgosho*), remained the effective ruler of Japan until his death. Ieyasu retired to [Sunpu Castle](/wiki/Sunpu_Castle "Sunpu Castle") in [Sunpu](/wiki/Shizuoka,_Shizuoka "Shizuoka, Shizuoka"), but he also supervised the building of [Edo Castle](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle"), a massive construction project which lasted for the rest of Ieyasu's life. The result was the largest castle in all of Japan. The cost of building the castle was borne by all the other daimyo, while Ieyasu reaped the benefits. The central [donjon](/wiki/Keep "Keep"), or *tenshu*, burned in the 1657 *[Meireki](/wiki/Meireki "Meireki")* fire. Today, the [Imperial Palace](/wiki/Tokyo_Imperial_Palace "Tokyo Imperial Palace") stands on the site of the castle.[[241]](#cite_note-260)

Edo became the center of political power and the *de facto* capital of Japan, although the historic capital of Kyoto remained the *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* capital as the seat of the emperor.[[167]](#cite_note-Sansom-181)[[242]](#cite_note-261) Furthermore, Ieyasu had the Imperial Court appoint his eldest remaining son, Hidetada, as Shogun, announcing to the world that the position of shogun would be hereditary to the Tokugawa clan from then on. At the same time, he requested Toyotomi Hideyori meet the new shogun, but Hideyori refused. In the end, the matter was resolved by sending his sixth son, Matsudaira Tadateru to Osaka Castle. At the same time, the next generation of Tokugawa clan vassals, including [Ii Naotaka](/wiki/Ii_Naotaka "Ii Naotaka") and [Itakura Shigemasa](/wiki/Itakura_Shigemasa "Itakura Shigemasa"), were also appointed.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurakawa2013120–121-262)

In 1608, Ieyasu assigned control of the [Tsu Domain](/wiki/Tsu_Domain "Tsu Domain")[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENakayama2015-263)[[245]](#cite_note-264)[[246]](#cite_note-Gaku-265) to Takatora. It was reported that the landholdings which Takatora received in Iga province had previously belonged to a lord named [Tsutsui Sadatsugu](/wiki/Tsutsui_Sadatsugu "Tsutsui Sadatsugu"). Ieyasu stripped Tsutsui Sadatsugu of ownership rights before giving them to Takatora.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETatsuo201860-266) The initial pretext was Sadatsugu's sloppy governance of the domain[[248]](#cite_note-267) however, historians have argued that the real reason was because Sadatsugu behaved suspiciously by visiting [Toyotomi Hideyori](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyori "Toyotomi Hideyori") at [Osaka Castle](/wiki/Osaka_Castle "Osaka Castle"), without approval of the shogun, while the land which Sadatsugu occupied was considered to be an important military strategic location.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE籔1985213–214-268) Furthermore, it is thought that Ieyasu expropriated the land and gave it to the Todo clan as political strategy against the Toyotomi clan, even though he was a patron of the Toyotomi family, Tōdō Takatora was considered a close ally of Ieyasu. Thus by putting him in control of portions of Iga province, the influence of the shogunate could be expanded to more strategic locations without directly provoking the Toyotomi faction in Osaka.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETatsuo201860-266)

In 1611, (Keicho 16), Ieyasu, at the head of 50,000 men, visited Kyoto to witness the [enthronement](/wiki/Enthronement_of_the_Japanese_Emperor "Enthronement of the Japanese Emperor") of [Emperor Go-Mizunoo](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Mizunoo "Emperor Go-Mizunoo"). While in Kyoto, Ieyasu ordered the remodeling of the Imperial Court, buildings, and forced the remaining western daimyo to sign an oath of fealty to him.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] On April 12, Ieyasu presented three articles of legislation to the daimyo in Kyoto. These Three Laws, as they were called, referred to the first shogun's set of laws and those that signed would strictly abide by the laws issued by the shogunate from then on. Second, the lords swore to not conceal those who disobeyed the shogun's orders, and third: to not hide or give shelter to any enemy of the state. 22 daimyo from the [Hokuriku region](/wiki/Hokuriku_region "Hokuriku region") and Western provinces agreed to the three articles of legislation and submitted an oath. Daimyo from Oshu and Kanto were not included in this list, because they were engaged in the construction of Edo Castle and did not come to Kyoto. In January of the following year, 11 major feudal lords from Oshu and Kanto swore to the Three Laws. 50 small and medium-sized fudai and tozama feudal lords also swore to the Three Laws, and Ieyasu succeeded in making all the feudal lords in the country his vassals.

Ieyasu did not have Hideyori work on national construction, nor did he have him swear to the Three Laws. Watanabe Daimon saw that the Three Laws issued by Ieyasu was a maneuver to isolate Hideyori politically by making all other influential daimyo lords obey him.[[250]](#cite_note-269)

In 1613, he composed the *[Kuge shohatto](/wiki/Kuge_shohatto "Kuge shohatto")* (公家諸法度), a document which put the court daimyo under strict supervision, leaving them as mere ceremonial figureheads.[[251]](#cite_note-270)

In 1615, Ieyasu prepared the *[Buke shohatto](/wiki/Buke_shohatto "Buke shohatto")* (武家諸法度), a document setting out the future of the [Tokugawa regime](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate").[[252]](#cite_note-271)

### Relations with Catholics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Relations with Catholics")]

Main article: [History of Roman Catholicism in Japan](/wiki/History_of_Roman_Catholicism_in_Japan "History of Roman Catholicism in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/William-Adams-before-Shogun-Tokugawa-Ieyasu.png/250px-William-Adams-before-Shogun-Tokugawa-Ieyasu.png)](/wiki/File:William-Adams-before-Shogun-Tokugawa-Ieyasu.png)

[William Adams](/wiki/William_Adams_(pilot) "William Adams (pilot)") before [shogun](/wiki/Shogun "Shogun") Tokugawa Ieyasu

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/KingJamesLetter.jpg/250px-KingJamesLetter.jpg)](/wiki/File:KingJamesLetter.jpg)

Letter from King [James VI of Scotland and I of England and Ireland](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") to ogosho Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1613

As Ōgosho, Ieyasu also supervised diplomatic affairs with the [Netherlands](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), [Spain](/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain"), and [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"). Ieyasu chose to distance Japan from European influence starting in 1609, although the shogunate still granted preferential trading rights to the [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") and permitted them to maintain a "factory" for trading purposes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From 1605 until his death, Ieyasu frequently consulted English shipwright and pilot, [William Adams](/wiki/William_Adams_(pilot) "William Adams (pilot)").[[253]](#cite_note-272) Adams, a Protestant[[254]](#cite_note-Nelson_2015_p._16-273) fluent in Japanese, assisted the shogunate in negotiating trading relations, but was cited by members of the competing [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") and Spanish-sponsored [mendicant orders](/wiki/Mendicant_orders "Mendicant orders") as an obstacle to improved relations between Ieyasu and the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church").[[255]](#cite_note-274)[[256]](#cite_note-275)[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurdochYamagata1903500-276)

In 1612, the [Nossa Senhora da Graça incident](/wiki/Nossa_Senhora_da_Gra%C3%A7a_incident "Nossa Senhora da Graça incident") occurred in Nagasaki, where the [bugyō official of Sakai](/wiki/Sakai_bugy%C5%8D "Sakai bugyō") Hasegawa Fujihiro had trouble with Portuguese captain André Pessoa.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoxer1951272–273-277) The conflict escalated when Pessoa and the merchants from [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") petitioned Ieyasu directly to complain about Hasegawa and [Murayama Tōan](/wiki/Murayama_T%C5%8Dan "Murayama Tōan"), a magistrate of Ieyasu. When the Jesuits learned of this affair, they were horrified when they found out about Pessoa's petition as they knew that Hasegawa's sister Onatsu was a favorite concubine of Ieyasu.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoxer194858-278)

Later, Pessoa withdrew his petition when he learned of the internal politics of the shogunate. However, Fujihiro refused to forgive Pessoa's petition.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoxer1948276-279) Fujihiro encouraged Arima Harunobu, who wanted to retaliate for the prior Macau incident, to petition Ieyasu for the capture of Pessoa and the seizure of his merchant ship.[[261]](#cite_note-Historist036263-280) Ieyasu, who had entrusted Harunobu with the purchase of [agarwood](/wiki/Agarwood "Agarwood"), was initially concerned that a retaliatory act would cut off trade with Portuguese ships.[[261]](#cite_note-Historist036263-280) Thus, the shogunate took a lenient attitude to Pessoa, as [Honda Masazumi](/wiki/Honda_Masazumi "Honda Masazumi"), with authorization from Ieyasu, gave Pessoa's envoy written assurances that Japanese sailors would be forbidden to travel to Macau, and any who did could be handled according to Portuguese laws.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoxer1951274Boxer194857-281) However, Ieyasu later gave Harunobu permission to capture Pessoa after he had been guaranteed that Manila ships of Spanish merchants would be able to replenish raw silk and other goods carried by Portuguese ships and also expected Dutch ships to continue arriving.[[261]](#cite_note-Historist036263-280) Then Ieyasu gave authorization to Hasegawa and [Arima Harunobu](/wiki/Arima_Harunobu "Arima Harunobu").[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoxer194858-278)

After several days of battle, which resulted in Pessoa's death, the remaining Portuguese merchants and missionaries were increasingly concerned about their fates, especially since Ieyasu had personally ordered their execution. Harunobu, who was Catholic, interceded on behalf of the Jesuits. Ieyasu changed his decision and the merchants were allowed to leave for Macau with their property. Ieyasu's Jesuit translator, [João Rodrigues Tçuzu](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Rodrigues_T%C3%A7uzu "João Rodrigues Tçuzu"), was replaced by William Adams.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMilton2011[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidjZqNl1GnkoYCpgPT38-282) Ieyasu then expelled João Rodrigues from Japan.[[264]](#cite_note-Takase_Koichiro_2002_383–384-283)

In 1612, the [Okamoto Daihachi incident](/wiki/Okamoto_Daihachi_incident "Okamoto Daihachi incident") occurred where Okamoto Daihachi (岡本大八, baptismal name Paulo), a Christian aide to the [rōjū](/wiki/R%C5%8Dj%C5%AB "Rōjū") [Honda Masazumi](/wiki/Honda_Masazumi "Honda Masazumi"), and Arima Harunobu, were implicated in series of crimes including bribery, conspiracy, forgery, and attempt to murder Hasegawa Fujihiro. Ieyasu was angered when he heard that Catholic followers had gathered at Okamoto's execution to offer prayers and sing hymns.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurdochYamagata1903498-284)

in 1614, Ieyasu was sufficiently concerned about Spanish territorial ambitions and signed the Christian Expulsion Edict. The edict banned the practice of Christianity and led to the expulsion of all foreign missionaries although some smaller Dutch trading operations remained in [Nagasaki](/wiki/Nagasaki "Nagasaki").[[266]](#cite_note-Mullins-285)

### Conflict with Hideyori

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Conflict with Hideyori")]

Main article: [Siege of Osaka](/wiki/Siege_of_Osaka "Siege of Osaka")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Hokoji%28rushanabutsu%29.jpg/250px-Hokoji%28rushanabutsu%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hokoji(rushanabutsu).jpg)

Replica of [Great Buddha of Kyoto](/wiki/H%C5%8Dk%C5%8D-ji_(Kyoto) "Hōkō-ji (Kyoto)") built by Hideyori

The last remaining threat to Ieyasu's rule was [Toyotomi Hideyori](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyori "Toyotomi Hideyori"), the son and rightful heir to [Hideyoshi](/wiki/Hideyoshi "Hideyoshi").[[5]](#cite_note-:0-9) He was now a young daimyo living in [Osaka Castle](/wiki/Osaka_Castle "Osaka Castle"). Many samurai who opposed Ieyasu rallied around Hideyori, claiming that he was the rightful ruler of Japan. Ieyasu found fault with the opening ceremony of a temple ([Great Buddha of Kyoto](/wiki/H%C5%8Dk%C5%8D-ji_(Kyoto) "Hōkō-ji (Kyoto)")) built by Hideyori; it was as if he prayed for Ieyasu's death and the ruin of the Tokugawa clan. Ieyasu ordered Hideyori to leave Osaka Castle, but those in the castle refused and summoned samurai to gather within the castle. Then in 1614, Ieyasu besieged Osaka Castle.

Tokugawa forces, with a huge army led by Ieyasu and *shōgun* Tokugawa Hidetada, laid [siege to Osaka Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Osaka "Siege of Osaka") in what is now known as "the Winter Siege of Osaka". Eventually, the Tokugawa were able to force negotiations and an armistice after cannon fire threatened Hideyori's mother, Yodo-dono. However, once the treaty had been agreed on, the Tokugawa filled in the castle's outer moats with sand so their troops could walk across. Through this ploy, Tokugawa gained a huge tract of land through negotiation and deception that they could not have gained through siege and combat. Ieyasu returned to Sunpu Castle, but after Toyotomi Hideyori refused another order to leave Osaka, Ieyasu and his allied army of 155,000 soldiers attacked Osaka Castle again in "the Summer Siege of Osaka".

In late 1615, Osaka Castle fell and nearly all the defenders were killed, including Hideyori, his mother (Toyotomi Hideyoshi's widow, Yodo-dono), and his infant son. His wife, [Senhime](/wiki/Senhime "Senhime") (a granddaughter of Ieyasu), pleaded to save Hideyori and Yodo-dono's lives. Ieyasu refused and either required them to commit seppuku, or killed both of them. Eventually, Senhime was sent back to the Tokugawa clan alive. With the [Toyotomi](/wiki/Toyotomi_clan "Toyotomi clan") line finally extinguished, no threats remained to the Tokugawa clan's domination of Japan.[[267]](#cite_note-286)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/The_tomb_of_Tokugawa_Ieyasu.JPG/250px-The_tomb_of_Tokugawa_Ieyasu.JPG)](/wiki/File:The_tomb_of_Tokugawa_Ieyasu.JPG)

The tomb of Tokugawa Ieyasu in [Nikkō Tōshō-gū](/wiki/Nikk%C5%8D_T%C5%8Dsh%C5%8D-g%C5%AB "Nikkō Tōshō-gū")

### Final year and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Final year and death")]

After the conflict with Toyotomi Hideyori, Ieyasu implemented the [Buke shohatto](/wiki/Buke_shohatto "Buke shohatto") code, which stated that each daimyo lord was only allowed to possess one castle.[[268]](#cite_note-287)

In 1616, Tokugawa Ieyasu died at the age of 73.[[10]](#cite_note-screech85-14) The cause of death is thought to have been [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer") or [syphilis](/wiki/Syphilis "Syphilis"). The first Tokugawa *shōgun* was posthumously deified with the name Tōshō Daigongen (東照大權現), the "Great Gongen, Light of the East". (A *[Gongen](/wiki/Gongen "Gongen")* is believed to be a [buddha](/wiki/Buddhahood "Buddhahood") who has appeared on Earth in the shape of a *[kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")* to save sentient beings). In life, Ieyasu had expressed the wish to be deified after his death to protect his descendants from evil. His remains were buried at the Gongens' mausoleum at Kunōzan, [Kunōzan Tōshō-gū](/wiki/Kun%C5%8Dzan_T%C5%8Dsh%C5%8D-g%C5%AB "Kunōzan Tōshō-gū") (久能山東照宮). Many people believe that after the first anniversary of his death, his remains were reburied at Nikkō Shrine, [Nikkō Tōshō-gū](/wiki/Nikk%C5%8D_T%C5%8Dsh%C5%8D-g%C5%AB "Nikkō Tōshō-gū") (日光東照宮), and that his remains are still there. Neither shrine has offered to open the graves, and the location of Ieyasu's physical remains is still a mystery. The mausoleum's architectural style became known as *[gongen-zukuri](/wiki/Gongen "Gongen")*, that is [*gongen*-style](/wiki/Gongen "Gongen").[[269]](#cite_note-288)

He was first given the [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") name Tosho Dai-Gongen (東照大權現), then, after his death, it was changed to Hogo Onkokuin (法號安國院).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] During this time, the chief physician who cared for him, Sotetsu Katayama, diagnosed Ieyasu with [stomach cancer](/wiki/Stomach_cancer "Stomach cancer"). However, Ieyasu misinterpreted Katayama's diagnosis of his illness as a tapeworm infestation. Thus, he did not take the medicine Sotetsu had prepared, and instead continued his own method of therapy which he believed could cure his perceived tapeworm problem. This resulted in Ieyasu's health continually deteriorating.

Although his son, Hidetada, also warned him about his medical method, this only served to anger Ieyasu, who arrogantly overvalued his own knowledge in the medical field. This conflict over his medical care ended when Ieyasu exiled Sotetsu to the Shinshu [Takashima Domain](/wiki/Takashima_Domain "Takashima Domain").[[270]](#cite_note-289)[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiyamoto1995-290)

At the time of his death, Ieyasu had an estimated personal wealth of about 4 million koku, which extended to 8 million koku for the total of the Tokugawa clan. He also possessed about 42 tons of gold as the Tokugawa shogunate had implemented the centralization of gold and silver mine ownership, unlike previous eras of government in Japan, in which possession of mine ownership was managed by local lords through the shogunate authorization.[[272]](#cite_note-Ieyasu_wealth-291)

The Tokugawa shogunate would rule Japan for the next 260 years.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-9)

## Personal information

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Personal information")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Tokugawa_Ieyasu_handprint.jpg/250px-Tokugawa_Ieyasu_handprint.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokugawa_Ieyasu_handprint.jpg)

Handprint of Tokugawa Ieyasu at [Kunōzan Tōshō-gū](/wiki/Kun%C5%8Dzan_T%C5%8Dsh%C5%8D-g%C5%AB "Kunōzan Tōshō-gū")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Ieyasu_precepts.jpg/250px-Ieyasu_precepts.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ieyasu_precepts.jpg)

[Precepts](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Translation:Ieyasu_precepts "s:Translation:Ieyasu precepts") on the secret of success in life drafted by Tokugawa Ieyasu from the collection of [Nikkō Tōshō-gū](/wiki/Nikk%C5%8D_T%C5%8Dsh%C5%8D-g%C5%AB "Nikkō Tōshō-gū")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Tokugawa_Ieyasu%2C_lifesize_statue%2C_1601_AD%2C_wood_-_Edo-Tokyo_Museum_-_Sumida%2C_Tokyo%2C_Japan_-_DSC06601.jpg/250px-Tokugawa_Ieyasu%2C_lifesize_statue%2C_1601_AD%2C_wood_-_Edo-Tokyo_Museum_-_Sumida%2C_Tokyo%2C_Japan_-_DSC06601.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokugawa_Ieyasu,_lifesize_statue,_1601_AD,_wood_-_Edo-Tokyo_Museum_-_Sumida,_Tokyo,_Japan_-_DSC06601.jpg)

Wooden statue of Ieyasu at Edo-Tokyo Museum.

A historical evaluation of Ieyasu by [Junji Mitsunari](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%85%89%E6%88%90%E6%BA%96%E6%B2%BB "jp:光成準治"), a historian on the faculty at [Kyushu University](/wiki/Kyushu_University "Kyushu University"), has compared Ieyasu's upbringing to that of another Sengoku era leader, [Mōri Terumoto](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Terumoto "Mōri Terumoto"). Mitsunari contrasted Terumoto's upbringing in the comfortable and stable domains of the [Mōri clan](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_clan "Mōri clan"), as opposed to the hardships Ieyasu faced during his childhood, in which he experienced instability in Mikawa province, spending much his youth as a hostage of other warlords; in his view these differences formed each leader's character, as reflected in the Sekigahara battle, where the indecisiveness of Terumoto cost the Western army greatly, while Ieyasu's bold decisions and his willingness to take risks gave him the edge during the war.[[273]](#cite_note-292)

John T. Kuehn saw Ieyasu as being capable of complex long game strategies, both politically and militarily, as shown during his conflict against Ishida Mitsunari. Kuehn saw the [Siege of Fushimi Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Fushimi_Castle "Siege of Fushimi Castle") as Ieyasu's deliberate strategy to sacrifice the castle and to bait Mitsunari into a set-piece battle, where his battle-hardened forces had an advantage over Mitsunari's forces.[[274]](#cite_note-293)

On the other hand, Kazuto Hongō has compared Ieyasu to Nobunaga as Ieyasu had insight to ensure the stability of the nation by the optimization and utilization of the abilities of his vassals rather than relying on the power of single individuals, as well as establishing succession systems.[[275]](#cite_note-Tokugawa_Vassals;_Kazuto_Hongo-294) [Tetsuo Owada](/wiki/Tetsuo_Owada "Tetsuo Owada") personally praised how Ieyasu managed to be quickly accepted by the citizens of the Kantō region and his ability to rule there without unrest after he transferred there, by continuing the lenient tax policies of the Hōjō clan in order to gain sympathy.[[276]](#cite_note-rekishikaido;_Ieyasu_Kanto-295)

Hamada Koichiro from [Himeji Dokkyo University](/wiki/Himeji_Dokkyo_University "Himeji Dokkyo University") has recorded that there is a systematic attempt of historiography studies in the aftermath of the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") to portray Ieyasu in a negative light as a "cunning old man" who used the temple's bell of Hokoji as [casus belli](/wiki/Casus_belli "Casus belli") to wage war against Hideyori. Koichiro saw this tendency of Ieyasu to resort to underhanded tactics as being quite justified to some extent for different reasons. One example was his attempt at suppressing the Mikawa Ikkō-ikki rebellion by breaking his promise of leniency to [Jōdo Shinshū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo_Shinsh%C5%AB "Jōdo Shinshū")'s followers in exchange for their surrender. However, Koichiro also points out that Ieyasu was also capable of bravery as showed by personally entering the battlefield during the rebellion, while also pointing out several anecdotes about Ieyasu as a youth showing promise as a capable military tactician when he still under the wing of the Imagawa clan.[[277]](#cite_note-296)

Tokugawa also known for his vindictiveness. It is said that Ieyasu once executed a man who came within his power because he had insulted him when Ieyasu was young.[[278]](#cite_note-297)

According to professor Watanabe Daimon, one of the biggest strengths of Ieyasu's policies was his benevolence towards his subordinates and his capability to forgive his enemies and even his own generals who betrayed him during the Mikawa Ikkō-ikki uprising. This allowed him to gain the loyalty of the Mikawa samurai clans. As an example, during the battle of Mikatagahara, those who shielded Ieyasu and even gave their lives to allow his retreat were the ones who once fought against Ieyasu in the past and had been pardoned.[[279]](#cite_note-IeyasuLoyaltyl;_Watanabe_Daimon.-298)

At the same time, he was also ruthless when crossed. For example, he ordered the executions of his first wife and his eldest son, a son-in-law of Oda Nobunaga; Nobunaga was also an uncle of [Hidetada](/wiki/Tokugawa_Hidetada "Tokugawa Hidetada")'s wife [Oeyo](/wiki/Oeyo "Oeyo").[[280]](#cite_note-299) According to George Sansom, Ieyasu was cruel, relentless and merciless in the elimination of [Toyotomi](/wiki/Toyotomi_clan "Toyotomi clan") survivors after the Siege of Osaka. For days, scores of men and women were hunted down and executed, including an eight-year-old son of [Toyotomi Hideyori](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyori "Toyotomi Hideyori") by a concubine, who was beheaded.[[281]](#cite_note-300) However, Ieyasu also known to be capable of forgiveness, such as how he gave pardon to [Watanabe Moritsuna](/wiki/Watanabe_Moritsuna "Watanabe Moritsuna") after he rebelled against Ieyasu during the Ikkō-ikki uprisings in Mikawa.[[282]](#cite_note-warandfaith;ikko;watanabe-301)

Unlike [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi"), he harbored no desires to conquer outside of Japan, only wanting to bring order, an end to open warfare, and to rule Japan.[[283]](#cite_note-302)

Ieyasu's favorite pastime was [falconry](/wiki/Falconry "Falconry"). He regarded it as excellent training for a warrior.

> "When you go into the country hawking, you learn to understand the military spirit and also the hard life of the lower classes. You exercise your muscles and train your limbs. You have any amount of walking and running and become quite indifferent to heat and cold, and so you are little likely to suffer from any illness."[[284]](#cite_note-303)

Ieyasu swam often; even late in his life he is reported to have swum in the moat of [Edo Castle](/wiki/Edo_Castle "Edo Castle").[[285]](#cite_note-304)

### Armors & weapons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Armors & weapons")]

Many sets of armors are said to have been used by Ieyasu, and at least 10 pieces of armor were used by Ieyasu during his life and recorded in history.

While warlords at the time preferred flashy armor and frontlets to show off their presence, the 'Hada Gusoku' armor used by Ieyasu in the Siege of Osaka is a jet black armor with very few decorations, and is said to represent Ieyasu's frugal personality.[[286]](#cite_note-Ieyasu_armor_exhibition-305)

Like Hideyoshi, Ieyasu also presented armor to Europe, and there is a record of the "Moji-i Domaru Armor" at Ambras Castle in Austria being a gift from "the Emperor and Empress of Japan to Holy Roman Emperor Rudolf II." This armor has the same characteristics as the aforementioned "Hanairo Hinomaru-i Domaru Armor" and armor presented by Hidetada to King James I of England in 1613, and is believed to have been presented by Ieyasu between 1608 and 1612. The armor has the characters for World (天下, *tenka*) on the front of the torso and left sleeve, and Peace (太平, *taihei*) on the back of the torso and right sleeve, woven with red thread.[[287]](#cite_note-306)

### Quotes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Quotes")]

> Life is like unto a long journey with a heavy burden. Let thy step be slow and steady, that thou stumble not. Persuade thyself that imperfection and inconvenience are the lot of natural mortals, and there will be no room for discontent, neither for despair. When ambitious desires arise in thy heart, recall the days of extremity thou hast passed through. Forbearance is the root of all quietness and assurance forever. Look upon the wrath of thy enemy. If thou only knowest what it is to conquer, and knowest not what it is to be defeated; woe unto thee, it will fare ill with thee. Find fault with thyself rather than with others.[[288]](#cite_note-307)

> The strong manly ones in life are those who understand the meaning of the word patience. Patience means restraining one's inclinations. There are seven emotions: joy, anger, anxiety, adoration, grief, fear, and hate, and if a man does not give way to these he can be called patient. I am not as strong as I might be, but I have long known and practiced patience. And if my descendants wish to be as I am, they must study patience.[[289]](#cite_note-308)[[290]](#cite_note-309)

It is said that he fought, as a warrior or a general, in 90 battles.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

He was interested in various [kenjutsu](/wiki/Kenjutsu "Kenjutsu") skills, was a patron of the [Yagyū Shinkage-ryū](/wiki/Yagy%C5%AB_Shinkage-ry%C5%AB "Yagyū Shinkage-ryū") school, and also had them as his personal sword instructors.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/100_views_edo_049.jpg/250px-100_views_edo_049.jpg)](/wiki/File:100_views_edo_049.jpg)

The familial temple of Tokugawa Ieyasu, Zōjō-ji, as depicted by [Hiroshige](/wiki/Hiroshige "Hiroshige") in 1857

Tokugawa Ieyasu was known for his devotion to the [Jōdo-shū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo-sh%C5%AB "Jōdo-shū") school of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") throughout his life, having been born into the [Matsudaira clan](/wiki/Matsudaira_clan "Matsudaira clan") which followed Jōdō Buddhism. As a way of demonstrating his constant favor towards the sect, he moved his familial temple to the [Zōjō-ji Temple](/wiki/Z%C5%8Dj%C5%8D-ji "Zōjō-ji") in [Edo](/wiki/Edo_(Tokyo) "Edo (Tokyo)") and funded massive renovations to older Jōdo temples, including the head temple of [Chion-in](/wiki/Chion-in "Chion-in") in Kyoto, while also financing the creation of several new temples. After confiding in the abbot of Zōjō-ji temple about wanting to become a deity to protect his country, he was given advice to regularly recite the [nembutsu](/wiki/Nianfo "Nianfo") for the purpose of being born into [Amida Buddha's](/wiki/Amit%C4%81bha "Amitābha") [Pure Land](/wiki/Pure_land "Pure land") of [Sukhavati](/wiki/Sukhavati "Sukhavati"), where he would be able to easily attain [Buddhahood](/wiki/Buddhahood "Buddhahood") and protect his descendants and the entire nation of Japan. Ieyasu readily accepted this advice, and constantly repeated the nembutsu until the day he died. Despite his personal devotion to Jōdo-shū, Ieyasu was not a strict sectarian, placing his secretary Denchōrō, a [Rinzai Zen](/wiki/Rinzai_school "Rinzai school") monk, in charge of all religious affairs in Japan, Buddhist and [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto") alike.[[291]](#cite_note-310)[[292]](#cite_note-311)

Later in life he also took to scholarship and [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), patronizing scholars like [Hayashi Razan](/wiki/Hayashi_Razan "Hayashi Razan").[[293]](#cite_note-312)[[294]](#cite_note-Goto-Jones_2009_p._38-313)

While at first tolerant of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Japan "Christianity in Japan"),[[295]](#cite_note-314) his attitude changed after 1613, and persecution of [Christians](/wiki/Kirishitan "Kirishitan") sharply increased. In 1613, Ieyasu sent the monk [Banzui'i](/wiki/Banzui%27i "Banzui'i") to [Kyushu](/wiki/Kyushu "Kyushu") to convert Christians there to [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Japan "Buddhism in Japan"), and in 1614 he completely banned [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[296]](#cite_note-315)[[297]](#cite_note-316) The hostility of Ieyasu towards Catholics was shown when he replaced Jesuit translator [João Rodrigues Tçuzu](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Rodrigues_T%C3%A7uzu "João Rodrigues Tçuzu") with [William Adams](/wiki/William_Adams_(samurai) "William Adams (samurai)") in his court.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMilton2011[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidjZqNl1GnkoYCpgPT38-282)[[264]](#cite_note-Takase_Koichiro_2002_383–384-283) This change of attitude is believed to be due to the [Okamoto Daihachi incident](/wiki/Okamoto_Daihachi_incident "Okamoto Daihachi incident"), where a Catholic daimyo and shogun's official were accused of a series of crimes.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurdochYamagata1903498-284) After the execution of Daihachi, Ieyasu reportedly said:

> If they see a condemned fellow, they run to him with joy, bow to him, and do him reverence. This they say is the essence of their belief. If this is not an evil law, what is it? They truly are the enemies of the Gods and of Buddha.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoxer1951318-317)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokugawa_Ieyasu&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

Ieyasu's life and accomplishments were used as a model for the Japanese statesman Lord Yoshi Toranaga, portrayed in [James Clavell](/wiki/James_Clavell "James Clavell")'s historical novel *[Shōgun](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun_(novel) "Shōgun (novel)")*. The 1980 [television miniseries adaptation](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun_(1980_miniseries) "Shōgun (1980 miniseries)") of the novel, starring [Toshiro Mifune](/wiki/Toshiro_Mifune "Toshiro Mifune") as Toranaga, and the [2024 miniseries](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun_(2024_TV_series) "Shōgun (2024 TV series)"), starring [Hiroyuki Sanada](/wiki/Hiroyuki_Sanada "Hiroyuki Sanada") as Toranaga, both used Ieyasu as a key reference.[[300]](#cite_note-SeriesOrder-319)[[301]](#cite_note-320)

2019 Netflix documentary Age of Samurai: Battle for Japan discusses the infighting in Japan during the late 16th century, or the Sengoku period, in detail. It starts with the ascension of Oda Nobunaga to how finally Ieyasu became the Shogun of Japan in 1600.

The [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK") show *[What Will You Do, Ieyasu?](/wiki/What_Will_You_Do,_Ieyasu%3F "What Will You Do, Ieyasu?")* (どうする家康, *Dousuru Ieyasu*; ***What Would You Do, Ieyasu?***[[302]](#cite_note-321))[[303]](#cite_note-322) is a [Japanese historical drama](/wiki/Jidaigeki "Jidaigeki") television series depicting a semi-fictional history of Tokugawa Ieyasu which starred [Jun Matsumoto](/wiki/Jun_Matsumoto "Jun Matsumoto") as Ieyasu.[[304]](#cite_note-323)[[305]](#cite_note-324)

Tokugawa Ieyasu appears as a leader of Japan in every *[Civilization](/wiki/Civilization_(series) "Civilization (series)")* game except *[Civilization V](/wiki/Civilization_V "Civilization V")*.[[306]](#cite_note-325)

The *[Age of Empires III: The Asian Dynasties](/wiki/Age_of_Empires_III:_The_Asian_Dynasties "Age of Empires III: The Asian Dynasties")* expansion pack has a Japanese campaign which focuses on the unification of Japan and the establishment of the Tokugawa shogunate with a fictional protagonist Sakuma Kichiro serving under Tokugawa Ieyasu.

Tokugawa Ieyasu appears in episode 63 of season 2 of *[Dinosaur King](/wiki/Dinosaur_King#Media "Dinosaur King")*.

1. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** 徳川 家康, Japanese pronunciation: [[to.kɯꜜ.ɡa.wa | i.eꜜ.ja.sɯ, -kɯ.ɡa.wa i.eꜜ-, -kɯꜜ.ɡa.wa (|) i.e-, -ŋa.wa-]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Ieyasu's given name is sometimes spelled **Iyeyasu**,[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5) according to the historical pronunciation of the [kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana") character *[he](/wiki/He_(kana) "He (kana)")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He was posthumously enshrined at [Nikkō Tōshō-gū](/wiki/Nikk%C5%8D_T%C5%8Dsh%C5%8D-g%C5%AB "Nikkō Tōshō-gū") with the name Tōshō Daigongen (東照大権現).
3. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** 松平 竹千代
4. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** He later took other names, which include **Matsudaira Jirōsaburō Motonobu**, **Matsudaira Kurandonosuke Motoyasu**, and finally, Tokugawa Ieyasu.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-20)** In addition, during this time of hostages in Sunpu, Hojo Ujinori of [Hōjō clan](/wiki/H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_clan "Hōjō clan") was also a hostage in Sunpu castle, so there is a theory that Ieyasu and Ujinori had been friends since that time, and this theory was included in the Dai Nihon Shiryō (*collection of historical documents from the ninth to the seventeenth century*) and other sources.[[15]](#cite_note-19)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-38)** Historians cannot reach consensus about the exact date when the alliance was officially proclaimed. Some have said the alliance was established two years after the Okehazama battle, although Ieyasu himself did not attend personally to the [Kiyosu castle](/wiki/Kiyosu_Castle "Kiyosu Castle"), since there are no historical records about his visit during this time.[[31]](#cite_note-36) Other theories from Akio Hirano deduced the alliance only formally announced in 1573.[[32]](#cite_note-37)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-41)** [Tetsuo Owada](/wiki/Tetsuo_Owada "Tetsuo Owada") stated the marriage between Nobuyasu and Tokuhime occurred in 1567, when both was nine years old.[[34]](#cite_note-Tetsuo_Owada_2002_24-40)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-62)** According to the Todai-ki, the castle was originally planned to be relocated to Mitsuke (the site of Kinosaki Castle is said to be), but was changed to Hamamatsu at the request of Oda Nobunaga. It is speculated that Nobunaga did not want the Oda and Tokugawa bases to be too far apart.[[54]](#cite_note-61)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-99)** During the [Sengoku period](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period"), particularly dangerous groups called ["Ochimusha-gari"](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E8%90%BD%E3%81%A1%E6%AD%A6%E8%80%85%E7%8B%A9%E3%82%8A "jp:落ち武者狩り") or "fallen warrior hunter" groups had emerged. These groups consisted of peasants or [rōnin](/wiki/R%C5%8Dnin "Rōnin") who had been dispossessed by war and now formed self-defense forces which operated outside the law, while in reality they often resorted to hunting and robbing defeated Samurai or soldiers during conflicts.[[89]](#cite_note-SAMURAI_HUNTER1-97)[[87]](#cite_note-SAMURAIHUNTER2-95)[[90]](#cite_note-SAMURAIHUNTER3-98) These outlaw groups were particularly rampant on the route which Ieyasu taken to return to Mikawa.[[90]](#cite_note-SAMURAIHUNTER3-98)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-112)** The name of "Tenshō-Jingo War" was coined by Tashiro Takashi in 1980.[[101]](#cite_note-110) Furthermore, there is also a theory that from the perspective that local powers which continued to fight over the possession of the Oda clan's leftover territories, there is evidence that Tokugawa Ieyasu's transfer to the [Kantō region](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region") following the fall of the Hōjō clan in 1590 and the placement of Toyotomi-line daimyo, until transfer of Uesugi Kagekatsu to Aizu, where the local daimyo were separated from their former territory and the establishment of control by the [Azuchi–Momoyama period](/wiki/Azuchi%E2%80%93Momoyama_period "Azuchi–Momoyama period"), was considered to be the extension of this conflict.[[102]](#cite_note-111)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-124)** the other two were said to be [Yamagata Masakage](/wiki/Yamagata_Masakage "Yamagata Masakage") and Sanada Masayuki.[[111]](#cite_note-rekishikaido;_Tensho-Jingo1-121)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-128)** The Tomohisa clan was a powerful clan that ruled the Ryuto area (left bank of the Tenryu River) in Shimoina during the medieval era. They were originally from Chikuzawa (modern day Minowa Town, [Kamiina District, Nagano](/wiki/Kamiina_District,_Nagano "Kamiina District, Nagano")) and were said to be one of the branches of the [Suwa clan](/wiki/Suwa_clan "Suwa clan"). During the Tensho-Jingo war, the Tomohisa was the ruler of [Chikudaira Castle](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%9F%A5%E4%B9%85%E5%B9%B3%E5%9F%8E "jp:知久平城"), located in Chikudaira, Shimo-Kuken, Iida City.[[116]](#cite_note-127)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-134)** Ieyasu's position and actions here are not those of an independent feudal lord, but as a feudal lord under the Oda regime, with the aim of defeating the Hojo clan[[121]](#cite_note-133)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-157)** At first, their members were 250 men. Later expanded to 500 after Ieyasu transferred into the [Kantō region](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region"). Later, they were appointed as guardians of Hachiōji castle, and their memberships expanded from 500 to 1,000. This is why they were called "Hachiōji sen'nin-dōshin" (Hachiōji's 1,000 officers.[[143]](#cite_note-:Hachioji_gang;_Tokugawa-156)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-203)** Historian [Andō Yūichirō](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%AE%89%E8%97%A4%E5%84%AA%E4%B8%80%E9%83%8E "jp:安藤優一郎") added, the true intention of Hideyoshi in transferring Ieyasu to Kantō was to weaken the power of the Tokugawa clan by moving them from their ancestral land in Mikawa, as he expected the former Hōjō vassals in Kantō would rebel against Ieyasu.[[182]](#cite_note-Ando_Yuichiro;_Tokugawa_Kanto-196) However, Kahara Toshi stated recent academic consensus is that this step by Hideyoshi would better viewed as a sign of his trust in Ieyasu's capability to rebuild the post-war Kantō.[[188]](#cite_note-Kahara_Toshi;_Tokugawa_Kanto-202)
16. **[^](#cite_ref-214)** Historian Kōichirō Hamada examined the historical records regarding the Korean invasion where Ieyasu expressed his eagerness to participate in this campaign. However, Hideyoshi organized to prioritize the daimyo lords from western provinces as vanguard which divided into 9 divisions, as he saw their positions were closer to Korea. Hamada stated by the fact that Korean invasion were dragged for years, there is good possibility that Ieyasu and other eastern province daimyo lords would be sent to Korea if Hideyoshi lived longer and the Korean campaign continues.[[198]](#cite_note-Ieyasu_Korea;_Kōichirō_Hamada-213)
17. **[^](#cite_ref-222)** historian Watanabe Daimon stated from the primary and secondary sources text about the accident this was more of legal conflict between those generals with Mitsunari, rather than conspiracy to murder him. The role of Ieyasu here was not to physically protect Mitsunari from any physical harm from them, but to mediate the complaints of those generals.[[205]](#cite_note-7_generals_Mitsunari;_Watanabe_Daimon-221)
18. **[^](#cite_ref-225)** Historians viewed this incident were not just simply personal problems between those seven generals against Mitsunari, as it was viewed as an extension of the political rivalries between the Tokugawa faction and the anti-Tokugawa faction which was led by Mitsunari. Following this incident, those military figures on bad terms with Mitsunari would support Ieyasu later during the conflict of Sekigahara between the Eastern army led by Tokugawa Ieyasu and the Western army led by Ishida Mitsunari.[[202]](#cite_note-7_generals_conspiracy;_Mizuno-218)[[206]](#cite_note-223) Muramatsu Shunkichi, writer of "*The Surprising Colors and Desires of the Heroes of Japanese History and violent womens*”, gave his assessment that the reason of Mitsunari failure in his war against Ieyasu was due to his unpopularity among the major political figures of that time.[[207]](#cite_note-224)
19. **[^](#cite_ref-255)** This predecent was found in a letter from Konoe Motohisa dated February 20, 1602 (Keicho 7). The circumstances surrounding Ieyasu's change of surname were similar with a previous case, when he changed from "Matsudaira" to "Tokugawa" in a letter dated December 3, 1566 (Eiroku 9) from Maehisa Konoe (Konoe Family Documents).[[236]](#cite_note-254)
